{"type":"stream_event","event":{"type":"content_block_stop","index":2},"session_id":"56d9ffc9-24ed-4173-9257-9d7110998b7f","parent_tool_use_id":null,"uuid":"35077cd1-f167-4218-baa9-5aa794a71bf5"}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_0a8547dd041d4596b062ff29","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# First-Failure Detection Logic\n2\t\n3\t**Bead:** adc-12bt — \"Design first-failure detection logic\"\n4\t**Child of:** adc-4vhr (Design first-failure tracking mechanism)\n5\t**Depends on:** adc-50ld (thread-safety approach — **closed**), and transitively on\n6\tadc-65l3 (data structure), adc-2duz (storage), adc-5xuy (comprehensive thread-safety spec)\n7\t**Status:** Design-only. Answers this bead's four acceptance criteria:\n8\t*(1) detection logic documented with pseudo-code/flow, (2) how \"first\" is determined,\n9\t(3) why subsequent failures are ignored, (4) edge cases (intermittent failures, config changes).*\n10\t**Date:** 2026-07-19\n11\t\n12\t> **Scope boundary.** This document owns the *detection logic* — the predicate that decides\n13\t> \"is this the first failure,\" *when* that predicate is evaluated, and the edge cases around it.\n14\t> It deliberately **does not** re-derive the thread-safety mechanism (adc-50ld), the storage\n15\t> location (adc-2duz), or the field set (adc-65l3); it consumes them. Where the detection logic\n16\t> intersects concurrency, it defers to the `asyncio.Lock` + await-free `_record_failure_locked`\n17\t> pattern already decided in adc-50ld/adc-5xuy.\n18\t\n19\t---\n20\t\n21\t## 1. What \"detection\" means here\n22\t\n23\tThere is one operational invariant (inherited from adc-4vhr / adc-50ld §2):\n24\t\n25\t> **Exactly one notification is emitted per process startup** when the Telegram bridge first\n26\t> fails; every subsequent failure is silent (DEBUG + counters only).\n27\t\n28\t\"Detection\" is the logic that decides, for a given failure, whether it is **the one** that\n29\ttriggers the notification. Three questions define it:\n30\t\n31\t1. **The predicate** — what condition makes a failure \"the first\"? (§2)\n32\t2. **The timing** — when in the send lifecycle is the predicate evaluated? Before send, after\n33\t   failure, or both? (§3)\n34\t3. **The win/lose semantics** — under concurrency, which of N simultaneous failures is \"first\"? (§4)\n35\t\n36\tThen §5 covers the check logic itself (pseudo-code + flow), §6 explains why losers are ignored,\n37\tand §7 is the edge-case catalog.\n38\t\n39\t---\n40\t\n41\t## 2. How \"first\" is determined\n42\t\n43\t\"First\" is **not** a timestamp comparison and **not** \"the earliest failure.\" It is defined\n44\toperationally as:\n45\t\n46\t> A failure is \"the first\" **iff it is the one that performs the `_has_logged_first_failure`\n47\t> False→True transition** inside the locked critical section.\n48\t\n49\tThe flag is the single source of truth (per adc-65l3). It starts `False` at startup\n50\t(`TelegramFallback.__init__`) and is monotonic within a process lifetime: it can only go\n51\t`False → True`, never back, except via explicit reset (§7.5) or process restart. Therefore:\n52\t\n53\t- The **first** failure is the unique failure that observes `_has_logged_first_failure == False`\n54\t  at the moment it holds the lock, sets it to `True`, stamps `_first_failure_timestamp`, and\n55\t  returns `was_first = True`.\n56\t- Every **subsequent** failure observes `_has_logged_first_failure == True`, increments\n57\t  `_failure_count`, updates `_last_failure_timestamp`, and returns `was_first = False`.\n58\t\n59\tThis is a **claim-and-set**, not a comparison. \"First\" = \"the winner of the claim.\" That is what\n60\tmakes it well-defined under concurrency (§4) and what makes the notification exactly-once without\n61\tneeding to deduplicate by time.\n62\t\n63\t---\n64\t\n65\t## 3. When to check — before send, after failure, or both?\n66\t\n67\t**Decision: detection is reactive — evaluated only after a send has actually failed. No pre-send\n68\tcheck on the send path.**\n69\t\n70\t### 3.1 The three places a check *could* go\n71\t\n72\t| Moment | What it would mean | Verdict |\n73\t|---|---|---|\n74\t| **Before send** (proactive reachability probe inline) | Call `check_bridge_available()` (or a lightweight HEAD) before every `send_message`, and treat a failed probe as \"first failure.\" | ❌ **Rejected** as the detection trigger |\n75\t| **After failure** (reactive) | Evaluate the predicate inside `_handle_send_failure`, which is reached only from `send_message`'s failure branches. | ✅ **Chosen** |\n76\t| **Both** | Probe-then-send, plus fall back to reactive on actual send failure. | ❌ **Rejected** — inherits both costs for no detection benefit |\n77\t\n78\t### 3.2 Why reactive-only (after failure)\n79\t\n80\t1. **A pre-send probe is not authoritative about send failure (TOCTOU).** A probe can succeed and\n81\t   the immediately-following send still fail (the bridge flaps in the gap), or the probe can fail\n82\t   and the send succeed (transient blip during probe). Using a probe as the \"first failure\" trigger\n83\t   would fire notifications for failures that never actually happened, and miss failures that did.\n84\t   The invariant is about *send* failures; only an actual send failure is evidence of one.\n85\t\n86\t2. **It adds a network round trip to the hot path for no gain.** `check_bridge_available()` does a\n87\t   full `httpx` GET to `/health` (5 s timeout). Running it before every `send_message` would double\n88\t   the per-send latency and add a new failure surface. adc-50ld §6 establishes the send path is\n89\t   dormant today; adding a pre-probe would manufacture exactly the kind of contention and latency\n90\t   the thread-safety design worked to avoid.\n91\t\n92\t3. **The \"proactive\" job is already done by a separate channel.** `src/main.py:150-162` already\n93\t   runs `check_bridge_available()` once during FastAPI `lifespan` startup and logs a WARNING if the\n94\t   bridge is unreachable, and `GET /api/v1/status/telegram_bridge` (`main.py:1469`) exposes\n95\t   `get_bridge_status()` for polling. Reachability *probing* is a health-check concern that feeds\n96\t   `_is_reachable`; first-failure *detection* is a send-path concern that feeds the flag. Conflating\n97\t   them would couple two different state objects — exactly what adc-50ld §5.4 warns against\n98\t   (\"one lock per logical state object\"; here, one *signal* per logical state object).\n99\t\n100\t> **So the lifecycle is:** startup probe (separate channel, informs `_is_reachable`) → sends\n101\t> proceed → **on the first send that actually fails**, `_handle_send_failure` runs the predicate\n102\t> reactively → notify-once → all later failures suppressed. The predicate is evaluated at exactly\n103\t> one point: inside `_handle_send_failure`, after a real failure has occurred.\n104\t\n105\t### 3.3 What \"after failure\" includes\n106\t\n107\t`send_message` (`src/telegram/fallback.py:64-94`) reaches `_handle_send_failure` from **three**\n108\tbranches, and the predicate treats all three as failure evidence today:\n109\t\n110\t1. Non-2xx HTTP response → `:84` (`status {code} - {text}`)\n111\t2. `httpx.RequestError` (connection refused, timeout, DNS, etc.) → `:89`\n112\t3. Any other `Exception` → `:93`\n113\t\n114\t> ⚠️ This breadth is a detection-logic sharp edge — see §7.3. All three currently count as \"first\n115\t> failure,\" but only branch 2 (and arguably transport-level 5xx in branch 1) actually indicates the\n116\t> *bridge* is down. Branch 1 with a 4xx is a per-message error, not a bridge outage.\n117\t\n118\t---\n119\t\n120\t## 4. Win/lose under concurrency (which of N failures is \"first\")\n121\t\n122\tWhen N sends fail near-simultaneously (e.g. a burst of dispatches all hitting a dead bridge), all N\n123\tcoroutines call `_handle_send_failure`. The predicate + the lock together guarantee exactly one\n124\twinner:\n125\t\n126\t- The lock (`asyncio.Lock`, per adc-50ld §5) serializes entry to `_record_failure_locked`.\n127\t- The first coroutine to enter observes `_has_logged_first_failure == False`, performs the\n128\t  False→True transition, and returns `was_first = True`. **It is \"the first.\"**\n129\t- Coroutines 2…N enter serially afterward, observe `True`, and return `was_first = False`.\n130\t- Net result: **exactly one notification**, `_failure_count == N` (the counter is a\n131\t  read-modify-write also under the lock — adc-50ld Race 2), one `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n132\t\n133\tThis is the detection-logic reason the check-then-act must be inside the lock: \"first\" is defined\n134\tby *who flips the flag*, and the flip must be atomic with the read that tested it. (The thread-safety\n135\tmechanics — why `asyncio.Lock`, why the await-free `def` helper — are adc-50ld's contribution; this\n136\tbead only asserts that detection *requires* the check-and-flip to be a single atomic step.)\n137\t\n138\t---\n139\t\n140\t## 5. The check logic — flow and pseudo-code\n141\t\n142\t### 5.1 Detection flow\n143\t\n144\t```\n145\tsend_message() attempted\n146\t        │\n147\t   ┌────┴───── actual send result ─────┐\n148\t   │                                    │\n149\tsuccess (HTTP 200)                  failure (non-2xx / RequestError / Exception)\n150\t   │                                    │\n151\t_is_reachable = True                  call await _handle_send_failure(error_context)\n152\treturn True                                  │\n153\t                                   ┌────────┴───── _first_failure_lock ────────────┐\n154\t                                   │  (await-free plain def: _record_failure_locked) │\n155\t                                   │                                                 │\n156\t                                   │  failure_count += 1                             │\n157\t                                   │  last_failure_timestamp = now                   │\n158\t                                   │  is_reachable = False                           │\n159\t                                   │                                                 │\n160\t                                   │  if has_logged_first_failure == False:          │\n161\t                                   │      has_logged_first_failure = True   ◄── claim│\n162\t                                   │      first_failure_timestamp = now             │\n163\t                                   │      was_first = True                          │\n164\t                                   │  else:                                          │\n165\t                                   │      was_first = False                         │\n166\t                                   └────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘\n167\t                                                │ (lock released; was_first captured)\n168\t                                   ┌────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┐\n169\t                                   │  if was_first:                                  │\n170\t                                   │      await _notify_first_failure(error_context) │ ◄── I/O outside lock\n171\t                                   │  else:                                          │   (side-channel; §7.4)\n172\t                                   │      logger.debug(...)                         │\n173\t                                   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n174\t```\n175\t\n176\tTwo structural rules from adc-50ld §5.5 that the detection logic depends on:\n177\t\n178\t- **The decision (`was_first`) is captured inside the lock; the notification I/O runs after\n179\t  release.** This is what lets the exactly-one guarantee survive even if the notification itself is\n180\t  slow or fails (§7.6).\n181\t- **`_record_failure_locked` is a plain `def` with no `await`.** Detection must not yield between\n182\t  the read of the flag and the write that flips it; the plain-`def` helper makes that mechanically\n183\t  impossible (adc-50ld §3).\n184\t\n185\t### 5.2 Pseudo-code\n186\t\n187\tConsistent with the sketch in adc-5xuy §5 and adc-50ld §5.5, focused on the *logic*:\n188\t\n189\t```python\n190\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n191\t    \"\"\"Reactive detection entry point. Called only from send_message failure branches.\"\"\"\n192\t    was_first = False\n193\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:                       # adc-50ld\n194\t        was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context) # plain def; no await\n195\t    # ---- lock released; detection decision is final below ----\n196\t    if was_first:\n197\t        await self._notify_first_failure(error_context)        # side-channel I/O (§7.4)\n198\t    else:\n199\t        logger.debug(\n200\t            f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count} \"\n201\t            f\"at {self.bridge_url}. Error: {error_context or 'unknown error'}.\"\n202\t        )\n203\t\n204\t\n205\tdef _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\n206\t    \"\"\"Caller MUST hold _first_failure_lock. Sync on purpose — no await.\n207\t\n208\t    Returns True iff THIS call is the first failure of the startup\n209\t    (i.e. this call performed the has_logged_first_failure False→True flip).\n210\t    \"\"\"\n211\t    now = datetime.now()\n212\t    self._is_reachable = False\n213\t    self._failure_count += 1\n214\t    self._last_failure_timestamp = now              # adc-65l3 name (current code: _last_failure_logged)\n215\t\n216\t    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:          # the predicate\n217\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = True       # the claim (atomic w/ the read, under lock)\n218\t        self._first_failure_timestamp = now         # set-once (field to be added — adc-65l3)\n219\t        logger.warning(\n220\t            f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n221\t            f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n222\t            f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n223\t        )\n224\t        return True                                 # winner → triggers notification\n225\t    return False                                    # loser → suppressed\n226\t```\n227\t\n228\t> **Naming note.** Current code (`fallback.py:43`) calls the last-failure field\n229\t> `_last_failure_logged` and updates it only on the first failure. adc-65l3 standardizes it to\n230\t> `_last_failure_timestamp`, updated on **every** failure, and adds the set-once\n231\t> `_first_failure_timestamp`. The pseudo-code above uses the adc-65l3 names. The detection logic\n232\t> does not depend on the rename; implementation should align names when it lands.\n233\t\n234\t---\n235\t\n236\t## 6. Why subsequent failures are ignored\n237\t\n238\tA failure that returns `was_first = False` triggers **no notification** and logs only at DEBUG.\n239\tThree reasons, in order of importance:\n240\t\n241\t1. **The core invariant is \"notify once.\"** Re-alerting on every failure when the bridge is down\n242\t   would spam the operator — the exact problem adc-4vhr exists to solve. The flag makes\n243\t   suppression the default for every failure after the first.\n244\t\n245\t2. **The flag is monotonic within a startup.** Once `_has_logged_first_failure == True`, no code\n246\t   path flips it back except explicit reset (§7.5) or process restart. So \"subsequent\" is a stable\n247\t   property: every failure from the second onward, for the rest of the process lifetime, is\n248\t   suppressed by construction — there is no timer to expire, no window to slide.\n249\t\n250\t3. **Diagnostics are not lost — only the noisy alert is.** Subsequent failures still:\n251\t   - increment `_failure_count` (visible at `/api/v1/status/telegram_bridge`),\n252\t   - update `_last_failure_timestamp` (\"how long has it been down?\"),\n253\t   - emit a DEBUG line with the failure number and error.\n254\t   An operator who wants the ongoing-failure picture reads `get_bridge_status()` or DEBUG logs;\n255\t   the notification channel is reserved for the one \"it just went down\" signal.\n256\t\n257\t> The design choice is deliberately **not** \"notify once, then never again even if it recovers and\n258\t> breaks.\" That stricter behavior is a consequence of the current once-per-startup semantic; §7.1\n259\t> and §7.5 cover the recovery/intermittent case and the reset that re-arms detection.\n260\t\n261\t---\n262\t\n263\t## 7. Edge cases\n264\t\n265\t### 7.1 Intermittent / flapping failures\n266\t\n267\t**Behavior under this design:** a bridge that fails, recovers, fails, recovers… produces\n268\t**exactly one notification** — at the first failure — for the entire process lifetime. Flapping\n269\tdoes not re-trigger.\n270\t\n271\t- **Why that's acceptable:** the operator gets the \"bridge is unhealthy\" signal once, at onset.\n272\t  Ongoing flap severity is still measurable via `_failure_count` (climbing) and\n273\t  `_last_failure_timestamp` (recent) on the status endpoint.\n274\t- **Why it might not be enough:** if the bridge flaps for hours after recovering between blips, the\n275\t  operator has no second alert to tell them \"it degraded *again*.\"\n276\t- **The intended extension point is recovery-based reset, not a time cooldown.** adc-65l3 §3\n277\t  specifies a future `_handle_send_success` that, after N consecutive successes, flips\n278\t  `_has_logged_first_failure` back to `False` — re-arming detection so the *next* degradation is a\n279\t  new \"first.\" That recovers the per-outage signal without time-based re-alerting. See §7.5.\n280\t\n281\t> ⚠️ **Reconcile the dormant constant.** `src/telegram/fallback.py:34` declares\n282\t> `FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS = 300` but it is **never referenced** anywhere (verified — only the\n283\t> declaration exists). It is a leftover from an earlier \"re-notify after a 5-minute cooldown\" idea\n284\t> that was superseded by the once-per-startup semantic (adc-65l3/adc-50ld). **Recommendation:**\n285\t> either delete it, or — if time-based re-notification is ever actually wanted — implement it\n286\t> deliberately against `_last_failure_timestamp` and remove this note. Do not leave a dead constant\n287\t> implying behavior the code does not have.\n288\t\n289\t### 7.2 Config changes (`ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL`)\n290\t\n291\t**Behavior today:** `bridge_url` is read from the env var exactly once, in `TelegramFallback.__init__`\n292\t(`fallback.py:36-40`). The singleton is created during `lifespan` (`main.py:152`) and lives for the\n293\twhole process. So a config change has no effect until the process restarts — and a restart resets\n294\tthe flag anyway, so detection and config stay consistent **by accident of the singleton lifecycle**.\n295\t\n296\t- **The latent risk:** if hot-reload of `bridge_url` is ever added (e.g. SIGHUP re-reads the env\n297\t  var and mutates the live singleton's `self.bridge_url`), the first-failure flag would become\n298\t  **stale relative to the new target** — the new URL's first failure would be suppressed because\n299\t  the flag is still `True` from the old URL's failure.\n300\t- **Detection rule for that future world:** detection state is logically keyed to the pair\n301\t  *(instance ↔ bridge_url)*. Any code path that changes `bridge_url` on a live instance **MUST**\n302\t  also call `reset_first_failure_state()` (§7.5), so the new URL gets its own first-failure window.\n303\t  Equivalently: prefer recreating the singleton over mutating `bridge_url` in place.\n304\t\n305\t### 7.3 Failure classification — 4xx vs 5xx vs transport (sharp edge)\n306\t\n307\tToday all three failure branches (§3.3) flip the flag identically. That means a **single 400 Bad\n308\tRequest** (a malformed message — a per-message application error, not a bridge outage) would \"use\n309\tup\" the one notification, and a subsequent genuine bridge-down event (503 / connection refused)\n310\twould be **suppressed**.\n311\t\n312\t- This is almost certainly not the intent: the invariant is about *bridge reachability*, and a 4xx\n313\t  says the bridge is up but rejected one payload.\n314\t- **Recommendation (detection-logic level):** scope \"first failure\" to **reachability-class**\n315\t  failures only — `httpx.RequestError` (transport) and 5xx/429 responses — and route 4xx to a\n316\t  per-message DEBUG path that does **not** touch `_has_logged_first_failure` or\n317\t  `_first_failure_timestamp` (it may still bump `_failure_count` under the lock, or not, per a\n318\t  separate decision). This keeps the one notification reserved for actual outages.\n319\t- This also aligns `_is_reachable`: today a 4xx sets `_is_reachable = False`, which is misleading\n320\t  for the same reason. Classifying before the handler fixes both.\n321\t- If the broad behavior is intentionally accepted for now, it must at least be **documented at the\n322\t  call site** so a future maintainer doesn't assume \"first failure\" means \"first outage.\"\n323\t\n324\t### 7.4 Self-failure / notification recursion guard\n325\t\n326\t`_notify_first_failure` must deliver the alert over a **side channel** — it must **not** call\n327\t`self.send_message(...)`. Reasons:\n328\t\n329\t- If the notify attempt fails and routes back through `_handle_send_failure`, it would bump\n330\t  `_failure_count` and overwrite `_last_failure_timestamp` with the *notification's own* failure,\n331\t  polluting the failure record with self-failures.\n332\t- There is **no infinite recursion** risk: by the time `_notify_first_failure` runs, the flag is\n333\t  already `True`, so any failure it triggers returns `was_first = False` and does not re-notify.\n334\t  But the state pollution is real and worth preventing structurally.\n335\t- Additionally, notifying over the *same bridge that just went down* is self-defeating — the\n336\t  notification would fail for the same reason the original send did.\n337\t\n338\t**Rule:** `_notify_first_failure` writes to a channel independent of the Telegram bridge — e.g.\n339\tstderr / a structured log sink / a different transport. The exact channel is out of scope for this\n340\tbead (it belongs to the notification-implementation bead), but the detection logic **requires** the\n341\tside-channel property and asserts it here.\n342\t\n343\t### 7.5 Reset re-arms detection\n344\t\n345\t`reset_first_failure_state()` (future, adc-65l3) flips `_has_logged_first_failure` back to `False`\n346\t(under the same lock) and clears `_first_failure_timestamp`, while **keeping** `_failure_count` and\n347\t`_last_failure_timestamp` for diagnostics. From the detection logic's perspective, reset means\n348\t**\"re-zero the predicate\"**: the next failure to claim the flag is \"first\" again and triggers a new\n349\tnotification. This is the mechanism behind both the recovery-based reset (§7.1) and the\n350\tconfig-change requirement (§7.2). It is also the test hook for re-arming detection in unit tests.\n351\t\n352\t### 7.6 Notification failure does not un-set the flag\n353\t\n354\tThe flag is set to `True` **inside** the lock, before release; `_notify_first_failure` runs\n355\t**after** release. Therefore if the notification raises, is cancelled, or its I/O times out:\n356\t\n357\t- The flag stays `True`. The next failure is \"subsequent\" (`was_first = False`) and will **not**\n358\t  re-attempt the notification.\n359\t- This is the desired exactly-once property, but it has a cost: **a lost first notification is gone\n360\t  until reset or restart.** There is no retry of the notify itself built into detection. (This\n361\t  matches adc-50ld §7: \"Only the best-effort alert is lost; the record was already written, so the\n362\t  one-WARNING guarantee holds.\") If notify-retry is ever required, it is a notification-layer\n363\t  concern, not a detection-layer one — and it must not be implemented by un-setting the flag.\n364\t\n365\t### 7.7 Concurrent first failures\n366\t\n367\tCovered by adc-50ld (Race 1) and §4 here: the lock makes the claim atomic; exactly one of N\n368\tconcurrent failures wins. Detection's only contribution is the *definition* of winning (the\n369\tFalse→True flip); the *mechanism* is the lock.\n370\t\n371\t---\n372\t\n373\t## 8. Acceptance criteria — mapping\n374\t\n375\t| Criterion | Where addressed |\n376\t|---|---|\n377\t| Detection logic documented with clear pseudo-code or flow | §5 (flow §5.1, pseudo-code §5.2) |\n378\t| Explains how \"first\" is determined | §2 (claim-and-set on the flag) + §4 (win/lose) |\n379\t| Explains why subsequent failures are ignored | §6 (three reasons) |\n380\t| Considers edge cases (intermittent failures, config changes) | §7.1 (intermittent), §7.2 (config), §7.3–§7.7 |\n381\t| Depends on adc-50ld completing thread-safety design | adc-50ld is **closed**; this design consumes its lock + `_record_failure_locked` pattern (§5) |\n382\t\n383\t---\n384\t\n385\t## 9. References\n386\t\n387\t- **Thread-safety (direct dependency):** `notes/adc-50ld-thread-safety-approach.md` — the\n388\t  `asyncio.Lock` + await-free `_record_failure_locked` pattern this detection logic runs inside.\n389\t- **Comprehensive spec:** `notes/adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` — the `_record_failure_locked` /\n390\t  `_notify_first_failure` sketch (§5) this doc's pseudo-code aligns with.\n391\t- **Data structure:** `notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md` — the field set\n392\t  (`_has_logged_first_failure`, `_failure_count`, `_first_failure_timestamp`,\n393\t  `_last_failure_timestamp`) and the reset/recovery semantics referenced in §2, §7.1, §7.5.\n394\t- **Storage:** `notes/adc-2duz-state-storage-design.md` — instance vars on the singleton,\n395\t  in-memory per-startup (why restart resets the flag, §7.2).\n396\t- **Current code:** `src/telegram/fallback.py` — `_handle_send_failure` at `:198`, the three\n397\t  failure branches at `:84/:89/:93`, dormant `FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS` at `:34`, singleton\n398\t  wiring in `src/main.py:152` and status endpoint at `main.py:1469`.\n399\t- **Downstream consumer:** bead adc-14la (\"Document complete first-failure tracking design\") will\n400\t  synthesize this detection logic with the data-structure, storage, and thread-safety designs into\n401\t  the end-to-end design for the implementation bead.\n402\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"56d9ffc9-24ed-4173-9257-9d7110998b7f","uuid":"d943155a-ce80-42e0-83d8-734615ec11b9","timestamp":"2026-07-19T22:43:30.068Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/notes/adc-12bt-first-failure-detection-logic.md","content":"# First-Failure Detection Logic\n\n**Bead:** adc-12bt — \"Design first-failure detection logic\"\n**Child of:** adc-4vhr (Design first-failure tracking mechanism)\n**Depends on:** adc-50ld (thread-safety approach — **closed**), and transitively on\nadc-65l3 (data structure), adc-2duz (storage), adc-5xuy (comprehensive thread-safety spec)\n**Status:** Design-only. Answers this bead's four acceptance criteria:\n*(1) detection logic documented with pseudo-code/flow, (2) how \"first\" is determined,\n(3) why subsequent failures are ignored, (4) edge cases (intermittent failures, config changes).*\n**Date:** 2026-07-19\n\n> **Scope boundary.** This document owns the *detection logic* — the predicate that decides\n> \"is this the first failure,\" *when* that predicate is evaluated, and the edge cases around it.\n> It deliberately **does not** re-derive the thread-safety mechanism (adc-50ld), the storage\n> location (adc-2duz), or the field set (adc-65l3); it consumes them. Where the detection logic\n> intersects concurrency, it defers to the `asyncio.Lock` + await-free `_record_failure_locked`\n> pattern already decided in adc-50ld/adc-5xuy.\n\n---\n\n## 1. What \"detection\" means here\n\nThere is one operational invariant (inherited from adc-4vhr / adc-50ld §2):\n\n> **Exactly one notification is emitted per process startup** when the Telegram bridge first\n> fails; every subsequent failure is silent (DEBUG + counters only).\n\n\"Detection\" is the logic that decides, for a given failure, whether it is **the one** that\ntriggers the notification. Three questions define it:\n\n1. **The predicate** — what condition makes a failure \"the first\"? (§2)\n2. **The timing** — when in the send lifecycle is the predicate evaluated? Before send, after\n   failure, or both? (§3)\n3. **The win/lose semantics** — under concurrency, which of N simultaneous failures is \"first\"? (§4)\n\nThen §5 covers the check logic itself (pseudo-code + flow), §6 explains why losers are ignored,\nand §7 is the edge-case catalog.\n\n---\n\n## 2. How \"first\" is determined\n\n\"First\" is **not** a timestamp comparison and **not** \"the earliest failure.\" It is defined\noperationally as:\n\n> A failure is \"the first\" **iff it is the one that performs the `_has_logged_first_failure`\n> False→True transition** inside the locked critical section.\n\nThe flag is the single source of truth (per adc-65l3). It starts `False` at startup\n(`TelegramFallback.__init__`) and is monotonic within a process lifetime: it can only go\n`False → True`, never back, except via explicit reset (§7.5) or process restart. Therefore:\n\n- The **first** failure is the unique failure that observes `_has_logged_first_failure == False`\n  at the moment it holds the lock, sets it to `True`, stamps `_first_failure_timestamp`, and\n  returns `was_first = True`.\n- Every **subsequent** failure observes `_has_logged_first_failure == True`, increments\n  `_failure_count`, updates `_last_failure_timestamp`, and returns `was_first = False`.\n\nThis is a **claim-and-set**, not a comparison. \"First\" = \"the winner of the claim.\" That is what\nmakes it well-defined under concurrency (§4) and what makes the notification exactly-once without\nneeding to deduplicate by time.\n\n---\n\n## 3. When to check — before send, after failure, or both?\n\n**Decision: detection is reactive — evaluated only after a send has actually failed. No pre-send\ncheck on the send path.**\n\n### 3.1 The three places a check *could* go\n\n| Moment | What it would mean | Verdict |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Before send** (proactive reachability probe inline) | Call `check_bridge_available()` (or a lightweight HEAD) before every `send_message`, and treat a failed probe as \"first failure.\" | ❌ **Rejected** as the detection trigger |\n| **After failure** (reactive) | Evaluate the predicate inside `_handle_send_failure`, which is reached only from `send_message`'s failure branches. | ✅ **Chosen** |\n| **Both** | Probe-then-send, plus fall back to reactive on actual send failure. | ❌ **Rejected** — inherits both costs for no detection benefit |\n\n### 3.2 Why reactive-only (after failure)\n\n1. **A pre-send probe is not authoritative about send failure (TOCTOU).** A probe can succeed and\n   the immediately-following send still fail (the bridge flaps in the gap), or the probe can fail\n   and the send succeed (transient blip during probe). Using a probe as the \"first failure\" trigger\n   would fire notifications for failures that never actually happened, and miss failures that did.\n   The invariant is about *send* failures; only an actual send failure is evidence of one.\n\n2. **It adds a network round trip to the hot path for no gain.** `check_bridge_available()` does a\n   full `httpx` GET to `/health` (5 s timeout). Running it before every `send_message` would double\n   the per-send latency and add a new failure surface. adc-50ld §6 establishes the send path is\n   dormant today; adding a pre-probe would manufacture exactly the kind of contention and latency\n   the thread-safety design worked to avoid.\n\n3. **The \"proactive\" job is already done by a separate channel.** `src/main.py:150-162` already\n   runs `check_bridge_available()` once during FastAPI `lifespan` startup and logs a WARNING if the\n   bridge is unreachable, and `GET /api/v1/status/telegram_bridge` (`main.py:1469`) exposes\n   `get_bridge_status()` for polling. Reachability *probing* is a health-check concern that feeds\n   `_is_reachable`; first-failure *detection* is a send-path concern that feeds the flag. Conflating\n   them would couple two different state objects — exactly what adc-50ld §5.4 warns against\n   (\"one lock per logical state object\"; here, one *signal* per logical state object).\n\n> **So the lifecycle is:** startup probe (separate channel, informs `_is_reachable`) → sends\n> proceed → **on the first send that actually fails**, `_handle_send_failure` runs the predicate\n> reactively → notify-once → all later failures suppressed. The predicate is evaluated at exactly\n> one point: inside `_handle_send_failure`, after a real failure has occurred.\n\n### 3.3 What \"after failure\" includes\n\n`send_message` (`src/telegram/fallback.py:64-94`) reaches `_handle_send_failure` from **three**\nbranches, and the predicate treats all three as failure evidence today:\n\n1. Non-2xx HTTP response → `:84` (`status {code} - {text}`)\n2. `httpx.RequestError` (connection refused, timeout, DNS, etc.) → `:89`\n3. Any other `Exception` → `:93`\n\n> ⚠️ This breadth is a detection-logic sharp edge — see §7.3. All three currently count as \"first\n> failure,\" but only branch 2 (and arguably transport-level 5xx in branch 1) actually indicates the\n> *bridge* is down. Branch 1 with a 4xx is a per-message error, not a bridge outage.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Win/lose under concurrency (which of N failures is \"first\")\n\nWhen N sends fail near-simultaneously (e.g. a burst of dispatches all hitting a dead bridge), all N\ncoroutines call `_handle_send_failure`. The predicate + the lock together guarantee exactly one\nwinner:\n\n- The lock (`asyncio.Lock`, per adc-50ld §5) serializes entry to `_record_failure_locked`.\n- The first coroutine to enter observes `_has_logged_first_failure == False`, performs the\n  False→True transition, and returns `was_first = True`. **It is \"the first.\"**\n- Coroutines 2…N enter serially afterward, observe `True`, and return `was_first = False`.\n- Net result: **exactly one notification**, `_failure_count == N` (the counter is a\n  read-modify-write also under the lock — adc-50ld Race 2), one `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n\nThis is the detection-logic reason the check-then-act must be inside the lock: \"first\" is defined\nby *who flips the flag*, and the flip must be atomic with the read that tested it. (The thread-safety\nmechanics — why `asyncio.Lock`, why the await-free `def` helper — are adc-50ld's contribution; this\nbead only asserts that detection *requires* the check-and-flip to be a single atomic step.)\n\n---\n\n## 5. The check logic — flow and pseudo-code\n\n### 5.1 Detection flow\n\n```\nsend_message() attempted\n        │\n   ┌────┴───── actual send result ─────┐\n   │                                    │\nsuccess (HTTP 200)                  failure (non-2xx / RequestError / Exception)\n   │                                    │\n_is_reachable = True                  call await _handle_send_failure(error_context)\nreturn True                                  │\n                                   ┌────────┴───── _first_failure_lock ────────────┐\n                                   │  (await-free plain def: _record_failure_locked) │\n                                   │                                                 │\n                                   │  failure_count += 1                             │\n                                   │  last_failure_timestamp = now                   │\n                                   │  is_reachable = False                           │\n                                   │                                                 │\n                                   │  if has_logged_first_failure == False:          │\n                                   │      has_logged_first_failure = True   ◄── claim│\n                                   │      first_failure_timestamp = now             │\n                                   │      was_first = True                          │\n                                   │  else:                                          │\n                                   │      was_first = False                         │\n                                   └────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘\n                                                │ (lock released; was_first captured)\n                                   ┌────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┐\n                                   │  if was_first:                                  │\n                                   │      await _notify_first_failure(error_context) │ ◄── I/O outside lock\n                                   │  else:                                          │   (side-channel; §7.4)\n                                   │      logger.debug(...)                         │\n                                   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\nTwo structural rules from adc-50ld §5.5 that the detection logic depends on:\n\n- **The decision (`was_first`) is captured inside the lock; the notification I/O runs after\n  release.** This is what lets the exactly-one guarantee survive even if the notification itself is\n  slow or fails (§7.6).\n- **`_record_failure_locked` is a plain `def` with no `await`.** Detection must not yield between\n  the read of the flag and the write that flips it; the plain-`def` helper makes that mechanically\n  impossible (adc-50ld §3).\n\n### 5.2 Pseudo-code\n\nConsistent with the sketch in adc-5xuy §5 and adc-50ld §5.5, focused on the *logic*:\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n    \"\"\"Reactive detection entry point. Called only from send_message failure branches.\"\"\"\n    was_first = False\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:                       # adc-50ld\n        was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context) # plain def; no await\n    # ---- lock released; detection decision is final below ----\n    if was_first:\n        await self._notify_first_failure(error_context)        # side-channel I/O (§7.4)\n    else:\n        logger.debug(\n            f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count} \"\n            f\"at {self.bridge_url}. Error: {error_context or 'unknown error'}.\"\n        )\n\n\ndef _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"Caller MUST hold _first_failure_lock. Sync on purpose — no await.\n\n    Returns True iff THIS call is the first failure of the startup\n    (i.e. this call performed the has_logged_first_failure False→True flip).\n    \"\"\"\n    now = datetime.now()\n    self._is_reachable = False\n    self._failure_count += 1\n    self._last_failure_timestamp = now              # adc-65l3 name (current code: _last_failure_logged)\n\n    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:          # the predicate\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = True       # the claim (atomic w/ the read, under lock)\n        self._first_failure_timestamp = now         # set-once (field to be added — adc-65l3)\n        logger.warning(\n            f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n            f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n            f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n        )\n        return True                                 # winner → triggers notification\n    return False                                    # loser → suppressed\n```\n\n> **Naming note.** Current code (`fallback.py:43`) calls the last-failure field\n> `_last_failure_logged` and updates it only on the first failure. adc-65l3 standardizes it to\n> `_last_failure_timestamp`, updated on **every** failure, and adds the set-once\n> `_first_failure_timestamp`. The pseudo-code above uses the adc-65l3 names. The detection logic\n> does not depend on the rename; implementation should align names when it lands.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Why subsequent failures are ignored\n\nA failure that returns `was_first = False` triggers **no notification** and logs only at DEBUG.\nThree reasons, in order of importance:\n\n1. **The core invariant is \"notify once.\"** Re-alerting on every failure when the bridge is down\n   would spam the operator — the exact problem adc-4vhr exists to solve. The flag makes\n   suppression the default for every failure after the first.\n\n2. **The flag is monotonic within a startup.** Once `_has_logged_first_failure == True`, no code\n   path flips it back except explicit reset (§7.5) or process restart. So \"subsequent\" is a stable\n   property: every failure from the second onward, for the rest of the process lifetime, is\n   suppressed by construction — there is no timer to expire, no window to slide.\n\n3. **Diagnostics are not lost — only the noisy alert is.** Subsequent failures still:\n   - increment `_failure_count` (visible at `/api/v1/status/telegram_bridge`),\n   - update `_last_failure_timestamp` (\"how long has it been down?\"),\n   - emit a DEBUG line with the failure number and error.\n   An operator who wants the ongoing-failure picture reads `get_bridge_status()` or DEBUG logs;\n   the notification channel is reserved for the one \"it just went down\" signal.\n\n> The design choice is deliberately **not** \"notify once, then never again even if it recovers and\n> breaks.\" That stricter behavior is a consequence of the current once-per-startup semantic; §7.1\n> and §7.5 cover the recovery/intermittent case and the reset that re-arms detection.\n\n---\n\n## 7. Edge cases\n\n### 7.1 Intermittent / flapping failures\n\n**Behavior under this design:** a bridge that fails, recovers, fails, recovers… produces\n**exactly one notification** — at the first failure — for the entire process lifetime. Flapping\ndoes not re-trigger.\n\n- **Why that's acceptable:** the operator gets the \"bridge is unhealthy\" signal once, at onset.\n  Ongoing flap severity is still measurable via `_failure_count` (climbing) and\n  `_last_failure_timestamp` (recent) on the status endpoint.\n- **Why it might not be enough:** if the bridge flaps for hours after recovering between blips, the\n  operator has no second alert to tell them \"it degraded *again*.\"\n- **The intended extension point is recovery-based reset, not a time cooldown.** adc-65l3 §3\n  specifies a future `_handle_send_success` that, after N consecutive successes, flips\n  `_has_logged_first_failure` back to `False` — re-arming detection so the *next* degradation is a\n  new \"first.\" That recovers the per-outage signal without time-based re-alerting. See §7.5.\n\n> ⚠️ **Reconcile the dormant constant.** `src/telegram/fallback.py:34` declares\n> `FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS = 300` but it is **never referenced** anywhere (verified — only the\n> declaration exists). It is a leftover from an earlier \"re-notify after a 5-minute cooldown\" idea\n> that was superseded by the once-per-startup semantic (adc-65l3/adc-50ld). **Recommendation:**\n> either delete it, or — if time-based re-notification is ever actually wanted — implement it\n> deliberately against `_last_failure_timestamp` and remove this note. Do not leave a dead constant\n> implying behavior the code does not have.\n\n### 7.2 Config changes (`ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL`)\n\n**Behavior today:** `bridge_url` is read from the env var exactly once, in `TelegramFallback.__init__`\n(`fallback.py:36-40`). The singleton is created during `lifespan` (`main.py:152`) and lives for the\nwhole process. So a config change has no effect until the process restarts — and a restart resets\nthe flag anyway, so detection and config stay consistent **by accident of the singleton lifecycle**.\n\n- **The latent risk:** if hot-reload of `bridge_url` is ever added (e.g. SIGHUP re-reads the env\n  var and mutates the live singleton's `self.bridge_url`), the first-failure flag would become\n  **stale relative to the new target** — the new URL's first failure would be suppressed because\n  the flag is still `True` from the old URL's failure.\n- **Detection rule for that future world:** detection state is logically keyed to the pair\n  *(instance ↔ bridge_url)*. Any code path that changes `bridge_url` on a live instance **MUST**\n  also call `reset_first_failure_state()` (§7.5), so the new URL gets its own first-failure window.\n  Equivalently: prefer recreating the singleton over mutating `bridge_url` in place.\n\n### 7.3 Failure classification — 4xx vs 5xx vs transport (sharp edge)\n\nToday all three failure branches (§3.3) flip the flag identically. That means a **single 400 Bad\nRequest** (a malformed message — a per-message application error, not a bridge outage) would \"use\nup\" the one notification, and a subsequent genuine bridge-down event (503 / connection refused)\nwould be **suppressed**.\n\n- This is almost certainly not the intent: the invariant is about *bridge reachability*, and a 4xx\n  says the bridge is up but rejected one payload.\n- **Recommendation (detection-logic level):** scope \"first failure\" to **reachability-class**\n  failures only — `httpx.RequestError` (transport) and 5xx/429 responses — and route 4xx to a\n  per-message DEBUG path that does **not** touch `_has_logged_first_failure` or\n  `_first_failure_timestamp` (it may still bump `_failure_count` under the lock, or not, per a\n  separate decision). This keeps the one notification reserved for actual outages.\n- This also aligns `_is_reachable`: today a 4xx sets `_is_reachable = False`, which is misleading\n  for the same reason. Classifying before the handler fixes both.\n- If the broad behavior is intentionally accepted for now, it must at least be **documented at the\n  call site** so a future maintainer doesn't assume \"first failure\" means \"first outage.\"\n\n### 7.4 Self-failure / notification recursion guard\n\n`_notify_first_failure` must deliver the alert over a **side channel** — it must **not** call\n`self.send_message(...)`. Reasons:\n\n- If the notify attempt fails and routes back through `_handle_send_failure`, it would bump\n  `_failure_count` and overwrite `_last_failure_timestamp` with the *notification's own* failure,\n  polluting the failure record with self-failures.\n- There is **no infinite recursion** risk: by the time `_notify_first_failure` runs, the flag is\n  already `True`, so any failure it triggers returns `was_first = False` and does not re-notify.\n  But the state pollution is real and worth preventing structurally.\n- Additionally, notifying over the *same bridge that just went down* is self-defeating — the\n  notification would fail for the same reason the original send did.\n\n**Rule:** `_notify_first_failure` writes to a channel independent of the Telegram bridge — e.g.\nstderr / a structured log sink / a different transport. The exact channel is out of scope for this\nbead (it belongs to the notification-implementation bead), but the detection logic **requires** the\nside-channel property and asserts it here.\n\n### 7.5 Reset re-arms detection\n\n`reset_first_failure_state()` (future, adc-65l3) flips `_has_logged_first_failure` back to `False`\n(under the same lock) and clears `_first_failure_timestamp`, while **keeping** `_failure_count` and\n`_last_failure_timestamp` for diagnostics. From the detection logic's perspective, reset means\n**\"re-zero the predicate\"**: the next failure to claim the flag is \"first\" again and triggers a new\nnotification. This is the mechanism behind both the recovery-based reset (§7.1) and the\nconfig-change requirement (§7.2). It is also the test hook for re-arming detection in unit tests.\n\n### 7.6 Notification failure does not un-set the flag\n\nThe flag is set to `True` **inside** the lock, before release; `_notify_first_failure` runs\n**after** release. Therefore if the notification raises, is cancelled, or its I/O times out:\n\n- The flag stays `True`. The next failure is \"subsequent\" (`was_first = False`) and will **not**\n  re-attempt the notification.\n- This is the desired exactly-once property, but it has a cost: **a lost first notification is gone\n  until reset or restart.** There is no retry of the notify itself built into detection. (This\n  matches adc-50ld §7: \"Only the best-effort alert is lost; the record was already written, so the\n  one-WARNING guarantee holds.\") If notify-retry is ever required, it is a notification-layer\n  concern, not a detection-layer one — and it must not be implemented by un-setting the flag.\n\n### 7.7 Concurrent first failures\n\nCovered by adc-50ld (Race 1) and §4 here: the lock makes the claim atomic; exactly one of N\nconcurrent failures wins. Detection's only contribution is the *definition* of winning (the\nFalse→True flip); the *mechanism* is the lock.\n\n---\n\n## 8. Acceptance criteria — mapping\n\n| Criterion | Where addressed |\n|---|---|\n| Detection logic documented with clear pseudo-code or flow | §5 (flow §5.1, pseudo-code §5.2) |\n| Explains how \"first\" is determined | §2 (claim-and-set on the flag) + §4 (win/lose) |\n| Explains why subsequent failures are ignored | §6 (three reasons) |\n| Considers edge cases (intermittent failures, config changes) | §7.1 (intermittent), §7.2 (config), §7.3–§7.7 |\n| Depends on adc-50ld completing thread-safety design | adc-50ld is **closed**; this design consumes its lock + `_record_failure_locked` pattern (§5) |\n\n---\n\n## 9. References\n\n- **Thread-safety (direct dependency):** `notes/adc-50ld-thread-safety-approach.md` — the\n  `asyncio.Lock` + await-free `_record_failure_locked` pattern this detection logic runs inside.\n- **Comprehensive spec:** `notes/adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` — the `_record_failure_locked` /\n  `_notify_first_failure` sketch (§5) this doc's pseudo-code aligns with.\n- **Data structure:** `notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md` — the field set\n  (`_has_logged_first_failure`, `_failure_count`, `_first_failure_timestamp`,\n  `_last_failure_timestamp`) and the reset/recovery semantics referenced in §2, §7.1, §7.5.\n- **Storage:** `notes/adc-2duz-state-storage-design.md` — instance vars on the singleton,\n  in-memory per-startup (why restart resets the flag, §7.2).\n- **Current code:** `src/telegram/fallback.py` — `_handle_send_failure` at `:198`, the three\n  failure branches at `:84/:89/:93`, dormant `FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS` at `:34`, singleton\n  wiring in `src/main.py:152` and status endpoint at `main.py:1469`.\n- **Downstream consumer:** bead adc-14la (\"Document complete first-failure tracking design\") will\n  synthesize this detection logic with the data-structure, storage, and thread-safety designs into\n  the end-to-end design for the implementation bead.\n","numLines":402,"startLine":1,"totalLines":402}}}
