{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_f79393339e624790ac81c92f","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# Thread-Safety Approach for Async FastAPI First-Failure State\n2\t\n3\t**Bead:** adc-50ld — \"Design thread-safety approach for async FastAPI\"\n4\t**Child of:** adc-4vhr (Design first-failure tracking mechanism)\n5\t**Depends on:** adc-2duz (state storage — closed), adc-5xuy (comprehensive design doc — closed)\n6\t**Status:** Authoritative parent-bead deliverable. Supersedes the prior version of this file\n7\t(dated 2026-07-08), which reached the right conclusion for the wrong reason and carried\n8\tperformance figures overstated by ~3,000–4,000×. **Where this document disagrees with the\n9\tprior version, this document is authoritative.**\n10\t**Date:** 2026-07-19\n11\t\n12\t> The full implementation-level specification lives in\n13\t> `notes/adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` (the comprehensive synthesis). This file is the\n14\t> concise parent-bead answer to the three acceptance criteria: *(1) thread-safety approach\n15\t> documented, (2) specific race conditions identified, (3) locking strategy explained or\n16\t> justified as unnecessary.*\n17\t\n18\t---\n19\t\n20\t## 1. One-sentence design\n21\t\n22\t> A single instance-level `asyncio.Lock` on the `TelegramFallback` singleton guards a\n23\t> minimal, **`await`-free** critical section over the first-failure record — because that\n24\t> state is in-memory, per-process, and per-startup. **The lock is defense-in-depth, not a\n25\t> fix for an active bug:** the current synchronous handler is already race-free on a single\n26\t> event loop.\n27\t\n28\t---\n29\t\n30\t## 2. What we are protecting\n31\t\n32\t`TelegramFallback` is a process-wide singleton (`get_telegram_fallback()`, first instantiated\n33\tduring the FastAPI `lifespan` at `src/main.py:152`). It upholds one operational invariant:\n34\t\n35\t> **Exactly one WARNING is logged per process startup** when the bridge first fails; all\n36\t> subsequent failures log at DEBUG.\n37\t\n38\tThe **first-failure record** is four instance attributes (per the data-structure bead\n39\tadc-65l3 and the storage decision adc-2duz — in-memory, per-startup, **no** persistence):\n40\t\n41\t```python\n42\tself._has_logged_first_failure: bool            # the check-then-act flag\n43\tself._failure_count: int                         # read-modify-write counter\n44\tself._last_failure_logged: datetime | None       # updated every failure\n45\tself._first_failure_timestamp: datetime | None   # set-once (to be added — adc-65l3)\n46\t```\n47\t\n48\tAll four are mutated in exactly one place today: `_handle_send_failure`\n49\t(`src/telegram/fallback.py:198`), reachable only through the async `send_message`\n50\t(`fallback.py:84,89,93`). A fifth attribute, `_is_reachable`, is shared mutable state but is\n51\t**deliberately out of scope** for this lock (§5).\n52\t\n53\t---\n54\t\n55\t## 3. The decisive nuance — why the current code is *already* safe (and why we lock anyway)\n56\t\n57\tThis is the single most important point in the design, and it refines what the prior version\n58\tof this file (and the race catalog in adc-4ol5) imply.\n59\t\n60\t**Fact about CPython asyncio:** the event loop runs one task at a time and can only switch\n61\ttasks at a suspension point — an `await` that actually yields. Between suspension points, a\n62\tcoroutine runs without interruption, including any *synchronous* function it calls. A\n63\tsynchronous function with **no `await` inside** is therefore atomic with respect to every\n64\tother coroutine on the loop.\n65\t\n66\tThe current `_handle_send_failure` is exactly that:\n67\t\n68\t```python\n69\tdef _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):   # sync, no `await`\n70\t    self._is_reachable = False\n71\t    self._failure_count += 1\n72\t    now = datetime.now()\n73\t    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n74\t        logger.warning(...)            # stdlib logging — blocking I/O, but does NOT yield\n75\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n76\t        self._last_failure_logged = now\n77\t    else:\n78\t        logger.debug(...)\n79\t```\n80\t\n81\tThere is **no suspension point** anywhere in this body. `datetime.now()` and stdlib\n82\t`logger.*` are synchronous and do not return control to the event loop. So two overlapping\n83\t`send_message` coroutines **cannot interleave** inside `_handle_send_failure` — the\n84\tT0/T1/T2/T3 \"both read False, both warn\" interleaving described in the race catalog cannot\n85\tfire as the code stands.\n86\t\n87\t**Therefore: the race described by the prior docs is *latent*, not *active*.** The invariant\n88\t\"one WARNING per startup\" currently holds because of *incidental* no-await atomicity, not\n89\tbecause of any explicit protection.\n90\t\n91\t**Why we add a lock anyway:**\n92\t\n93\t| Role of the lock | What it buys |\n94\t|---|---|\n95\t| **Defense-in-depth against future `await`s** | The moment a maintainer adds an `await` inside the critical section (async logging handler, a DB persist, an inline notification call), the incidental atomicity evaporates *silently* and the latent race goes live. An explicit lock makes correctness survive that change. |\n96\t| **Explicit, self-documenting contract** | \"No `await` in here\" is an invisible convention. `async with self._first_failure_lock:` makes the critical section's boundaries visible to every reader and reviewer. |\n97\t| **Negligible cost** | Measured ~0.32 µs of isolated overhead (adc-4rh3), on a path that is **dormant today** (see §6). There is no performance reason *not* to take the insurance. |\n98\t\n99\t> **The honest answer to \"is a lock needed?\":** Strictly, no — the synchronous, await-less\n100\t> handler is already atomic on this single-worker, single-loop deployment. We add the lock so\n101\t> that correctness does not depend on a future maintainer knowing that, and so that the\n102\t> notification I/O the design wants to add can be wired in safely.\n103\t\n104\t---\n105\t\n106\t## 4. Race conditions identified\n107\t\n108\tSix races arise when concurrent coroutines enter the failure handler. Cataloged in full in\n109\t`notes/adc-4ol5-race-conditions.md`; the decisive ones:\n110\t\n111\t| # | Race | Pattern | Severity | Status today |\n112\t|---|---|---|---|---|\n113\t| 1 | **Duplicate first-failure WARNINGs** | check-then-act on `_has_logged_first_failure` | **High** — breaks the core invariant | Latent (no `await` to interleave) → **goes live the moment an `await` enters the section** |\n114\t| 2 | **Lost counter updates** | read-modify-write `_failure_count += 1` (LOAD/ADD/STORE) | Medium | Latent for the same reason |\n115\t| 3 | **First-failure timestamp overwrite** | set-once via the racy flag check | Low | Latent |\n116\t| 4 | **Last-failure timestamp lost update** | non-deterministic write ordering | Low | Latent |\n117\t| 5 | **Read-during-write on status check** | `get_bridge_status` reads mid-mutation | Low | Tolerated (stale-but-consistent is fine for monitoring) |\n118\t| 6 | **Reachability toggle** | concurrent success (`_is_reachable = True`) vs failure (`= False`) | Medium | Accepted as benign (single-bytecode writes, last-write-wins) — see §5 |\n119\t\n120\t**The dividing line is the *shape* of the operation, not the field:**\n121\t\n122\t| Shape | Example | Atomic on a single loop? |\n123\t|---|---|---|\n124\t| Single assignment of an immutable | `_has_logged_first_failure = True` | ✅ atomic (one bytecode, and GIL-protected) |\n125\t| Read of a single field | `_failure_count` in `get_bridge_status` | ✅ atomic |\n126\t| **Check-then-act** | `if not flag: flag = True` | ⚠️ atomic *only while there's no `await` between check and act* |\n127\t| **Read-modify-write** | `_failure_count += 1` | ⚠️ atomic *only while there's no `await` between read and write* |\n128\t| Multi-field consistent snapshot | status dict assembled field-by-field | ⚠️ same caveat |\n129\t\n130\tThe GIL makes single bytecodes atomic; it does **not** make compound sequences atomic, and\n131\tasyncio does not make them atomic either — it merely happens not to interrupt them absent a\n132\tsuspension point. That caveat is precisely what the lock removes.\n133\t\n134\t---\n135\t\n136\t## 5. Locking strategy\n137\t\n138\t### 5.1 Decision\n139\t\n140\t> **One instance-level `asyncio.Lock` per `TelegramFallback`, created in `__init__`, scoped to\n141\t> the first-failure record fields only.**\n142\t\n143\t- **All mutations** of the record go through `async with self._first_failure_lock:`.\n144\t- **Reads** (`get_bridge_status`) take **no** lock — single-field atomic reads; monitoring\n145\t  tolerates staleness (Race 5).\n146\t- The critical section contains **no `await`/I/O** — implemented as a pre-locked **plain `def`\n147\t  helper** so I/O is structurally impossible inside it.\n148\t\n149\t### 5.2 Why `asyncio.Lock` and not the alternatives\n150\t\n151\tThe right mechanism is dictated by **where the state lives and which concurrency layer touches\n152\tit.** For this state only the event-loop layer exists (single worker, all-async access paths,\n153\tin-memory per-startup — verified against CLAUDE.md and `src/`):\n154\t\n155\t| Mechanism | Verdict | Why |\n156\t|---|---|---|\n157\t| **`asyncio.Lock` (instance)** | ✅ **Chosen** | Native to async, non-blocking, sub-µs uncontended, groups the multi-field record into one consistent section. |\n158\t| `threading.Lock` | ❌ wrong layer | Blocking acquire stalls the loop; can't be held across `await`. No threaded path touches this state. |\n159\t| Module-level / `app.state` lock | ⚠️ unnecessary | State is owned by the singleton instance; an instance lock is better encapsulated. |\n160\t| Queue-based serialization | ❌ overkill | A background task + queue lifecycle for one boolean flag. |\n161\t\n162\t### 5.3 Correction: \"atomic operations are NOT POSSIBLE\" is false\n163\t\n164\tThe prior version of this file (Option 4) declared atomic operations impossible in Python and\n165\tconcluded `asyncio.Lock` is the *only* viable mechanism. That reasoning is **wrong**, and the\n166\tcorrection matters for future maintainers:\n167\t\n168\t- **GIL-level atomics** exist for single-bytecode operations (§4 table).\n169\t- **SQLite transactions are atomic** — strictly *stronger* than an in-memory lock for\n170\t  persisted state (survive restarts, span workers). `src/session/store.py` already runs\n171\t  `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`, and `aiosqlite` is already a dependency.\n172\t- **File-level atomics** (`os.replace` temp-then-rename) exist for on-disk state.\n173\t\n174\tThe right mental model is **pick the mechanism that matches where the state lives**, not\n175\t\"always reach for `asyncio.Lock`.\" For the first-failure record the *conclusion* (use\n176\t`asyncio.Lock`) is correct — but the *reason* is \"because this state is in-memory,\n177\tper-process, per-startup,\" **not** \"because atomics are impossible.\" Recording the right reason\n178\tprevents a future maintainer from mis-generalizing and blocking a better design elsewhere\n179\t(e.g. a genuine `find_or_create_topic` TOCTOU, which should be fixed with a unique index +\n180\t`INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING … RETURNING`, not a lock).\n181\t\n182\t### 5.4 `_is_reachable` is explicitly out of scope\n183\t\n184\t`_is_reachable` is written from *different* paths (success at `:80`, health-check at `:176,179`,\n185\tfailure at `:207`). **Do not pull it under the first-failure lock:** every write is a single\n186\tatomic `STORE_ATTR` with no check-then-act, it has a different lifecycle, and coupling it to a\n187\tfailure-path lock would force the success/health-check paths to acquire a failure lock for no\n188\treason. Rule of thumb: **one lock per logical state object.**\n189\t\n190\t### 5.5 The structural rules (the parts a reviewer must enforce)\n191\t\n192\t1. **One `asyncio.Lock`** per instance, created in `__init__` (safe — singleton built on the\n193\t   serving loop, constructor does not `await`).\n194\t2. **No `await` inside the critical section.** Enforce by putting the body in a plain `def`\n195\t   helper (`_record_failure_locked`) — a `def` mechanically cannot `await`.\n196\t3. **I/O runs after release**, keyed on a `was_first: bool` captured inside the section.\n197\t4. **Never nest two `async with self._first_failure_lock:`** — `asyncio.Lock` is non-reentrant;\n198\t   re-entry deadlocks. There is no async `RLock`; the pre-locked helper is the fix.\n199\t5. **No lock timeout** — the section is await-free, so a holder can only be preempted between\n200\t   bytecodes for microseconds; a timeout would add a failure mode with no good recovery.\n201\t6. **`get_bridge_status` and all `_is_reachable` writes stay lock-free.**\n202\t\n203\tSketch (full version + diff in `adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` §5–§6):\n204\t\n205\t```python\n206\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n207\t    was_first = False\n208\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n209\t        was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context)  # plain def; no await\n210\t    if was_first:\n211\t        await self._notify_first_failure(error_context)         # I/O outside the lock\n212\t\n213\tdef _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\n214\t    \"\"\"Caller MUST hold _first_failure_lock. Sync on purpose — no await.\"\"\"\n215\t    ...\n216\t```\n217\t\n218\t---\n219\t\n220\t## 6. Performance (corrected)\n221\t\n222\tMeasured on CPython 3.12 on this box (full benchmark + listing in adc-4rh3; logging raised to\n223\tCRITICAL to isolate lock cost from handler I/O):\n224\t\n225\t| Operation | Measured cost |\n226\t|---|---|\n227\t| Uncontended `async with lock:` (empty body) | **~0.47 µs** |\n228\t| Lock-free status read | **~0.09 µs** |\n229\t| Isolated lock overhead (realistic section, with − without) | **~0.32 µs** |\n230\t| 100 fully-serialized failures | **~60–90 µs total** (count exactly correct; flat to 128 contenders) |\n231\t\n232\t> **Correction to the prior version of this file.** It carried \"~1–2 ms per failure\",\n233\t> \"2–5 ms per request\", and \"100 concurrent failures = 200 ms total\" figures. Measurement\n234\t> shows the lock itself costs ~0.32 µs and 100 serialized failures complete in **~60–90 µs**.\n235\t> The prior figures are **overstated by ~3,000–4,000×** — they conflated lock acquisition cost\n236\t> (sub-µs) with logging-I/O latency (ms, only if a handler does I/O). **Do not propagate them.**\n237\t\n238\tThe decisive argument is grounded in the code, not the microbenchmark: the protected path is\n239\t**dormant today.** `_handle_send_failure` is reachable only via `send_message`, and\n240\t`send_message`/`send_result` have **no live callers** outside `fallback.py` (verified across\n241\t`src/` — `send_exception` and `send_workload_summary` are no-op stubs). The only live touchers\n242\tof the singleton are the lifespan health-check (writes `_is_reachable`, not the record) and the\n243\tread-only status endpoint. **You cannot contend a lock on a code path that isn't called.**\n244\t\n245\tThe one real performance risk is **I/O inside the critical section** (e.g. calling a 10 s-timeout\n246\tnotification `await` inside the lock → every concurrent failing request serializes behind one\n247\t10 s timeout). That is structurally prevented by the plain-`def` helper (§5.5 rule 2).\n248\t\n249\t---\n250\t\n251\t## 7. Edge cases (summary)\n252\t\n253\t| Case | Resolution |\n254\t|---|---|\n255\t| Lock timeout | None added (§5.5 rule 5). If ever added, on-timeout behavior must be \"log + skip the update,\" never a partial write. |\n256\t| Deadlock / self-reentrancy | Prevented: single lock + pre-locked `def` helper; review rule \"never nest two `async with` on the same lock.\" |\n257\t| Cancellation while waiting / holding | `async with` releases on `CancelledError`; body never runs or runs atomically; lock never leaked. |\n258\t| Cancellation during post-lock notification | Only the (best-effort) alert is lost; the record was already written, so the one-WARNING guarantee holds. |\n259\t| Exception inside the section | `async with` releases the lock; read the decision first, write last so an exception is most likely before any field is touched. |\n260\t| Reset vs. in-flight failure | Both `reset_first_failure_state` and `_handle_send_failure` take the same lock → they serialize cleanly; no contradictory state. |\n261\t| Lazy-singleton init | `get_telegram_fallback()` is safe because `__init__` does not `await` — no yield between the `is None` check and the assignment. Would need to move into `lifespan` if `__init` ever awaits. |\n262\t| Multi-worker / restart | In-memory lock does not coordinate across workers or after restart. Acceptable: deployment is single-worker, and the semantic is \"one WARNING **per startup**.\" If it becomes \"one per **deployment**,\" escalate to the SQLite-atomic CAS (§8). |\n263\t\n264\t---\n265\t\n266\t## 8. Upgrade path (when atomics become the right answer)\n267\t\n268\tSwitch the record to the SQLite-atomic pattern (`UPDATE … SET claimed=1, first_failure_at=? WHERE\n269\tid=1 AND claimed=0` + `cur.rowcount == 1` as the compare-and-set; `aiosqlite` already a dep, no\n270\tnew deps) **if any of these becomes true:**\n271\t\n272\t- State must survive restarts (one first-failure per deployment, not per startup).\n273\t- adc is scaled to `uvicorn --workers N`.\n274\t- A `def` route or threadpool path starts touching this state.\n275\t\n276\tThis is a **correctness/durability** escalation (a SQL round-trip is ~0.1–1 ms vs ~0.32 µs for the\n277\tlock), not a performance one. Recorded so it need not be re-derived.\n278\t\n279\t---\n280\t\n281\t## 9. Verification pointers\n282\t\n283\tTests to add alongside the implementation (details in adc-5xuy §10 / adc-4rh3 §11):\n284\t\n285\t- **Concurrent first-failure:** `asyncio.gather` of N `_handle_send_failure` → exactly one\n286\t  WARNING, `failure_count == N`, single `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n287\t- **Counter accuracy:** 100 concurrent calls → `failure_count == 100`.\n288\t- **Structural no-I/O guard:** `assert inspect.iscoroutinefunction(fallback._record_failure_locked)\n289\t  is False` — the load-bearing invariant.\n290\t- **Reset-vs-failure** and **read-doesn't-block** tests.\n291\t\n292\t---\n293\t\n294\t## 10. Corrections logged vs. the prior version of this file\n295\t\n296\t| Prior claim (2026-07-08) | Status | Why |\n297\t|---|---|---|\n298\t| \"Atomic operations NOT POSSIBLE\" (Option 4) | **Wrong** | GIL atomics, SQLite txns, and `os.replace` all exist. Use `asyncio.Lock` because the state is in-memory per-startup, not because atomics are impossible. |\n299\t| \"~2–5 ms per request\", \"100 failures = 200 ms\" | **Overstated ~3,000–4,000×** | Measured ~0.32 µs lock overhead; 100 failures ≈ 60–90 µs. Conflated lock cost with logging-I/O latency. |\n300\t| Current code \"breaks the moment two requests fail in overlapping coroutines\" | **Imprecise** | Overlapping coroutines do **not** interleave inside a synchronous, await-less function on a single event loop. The race is **latent**, held off by incidental no-await atomicity. The lock makes the property robust rather than incidental. |\n301\t| `asyncio.Lock` recommended (Option 1) | **Correct** | Conclusion stands; reasoning refined. |\n302\t| Race catalog (duplicate WARNING, lost counter, timestamp overwrite) | **Correct** | Unchanged; reframed as latent. |\n303\t\n304\t---\n305\t\n306\t## References\n307\t\n308\t- **Comprehensive implementation spec:** `notes/adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` (adc-5xuy) — the\n309\t  deep dive; concrete diff, reviewer checklist, cheat sheet.\n310\t- **Race catalog:** `notes/adc-4ol5-race-conditions.md` (adc-4ol5).\n311\t- **Mechanism research:** `notes/adc-4783.md` (adc-4783) — `asyncio.Lock` behavior, GIL atomicity,\n312\t  SQLite-atomic CAS pattern, correction to \"atomics impossible.\"\n313\t- **Strategy decision:** `notes/adc-41u0.md` (adc-41u0).\n314\t- **Performance analysis:** `notes/adc-4rh3.md` (adc-4rh3) — measured costs; benchmark at\n315\t  `~/scratch/adc-4rh3-lock-bench.py`.\n316\t- **Data structure / storage (dependencies):** `notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md`\n317\t  (adc-65l3), `notes/adc-2duz-state-storage-design.md` (adc-2duz) — storage decision is\n318\t  in-memory, per-startup, no persistence.\n319\t- **Current code:** `src/telegram/fallback.py` — `_handle_send_failure` at `:198`, call sites at\n320\t  `:84,89,93`, `__init__` at `:36`; singleton created in `lifespan` at `src/main.py:152`; status\n321\t  read at `src/main.py:1474`. Deployment: single worker (`uvicorn src.main:app`, no `--workers`).\n322\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"56d9ffc9-24ed-4173-9257-9d7110998b7f","uuid":"d0aae3f5-6a83-421c-ae83-e9cc144a0646","timestamp":"2026-07-19T22:43:30.142Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/notes/adc-50ld-thread-safety-approach.md","content":"# Thread-Safety Approach for Async FastAPI First-Failure State\n\n**Bead:** adc-50ld — \"Design thread-safety approach for async FastAPI\"\n**Child of:** adc-4vhr (Design first-failure tracking mechanism)\n**Depends on:** adc-2duz (state storage — closed), adc-5xuy (comprehensive design doc — closed)\n**Status:** Authoritative parent-bead deliverable. Supersedes the prior version of this file\n(dated 2026-07-08), which reached the right conclusion for the wrong reason and carried\nperformance figures overstated by ~3,000–4,000×. **Where this document disagrees with the\nprior version, this document is authoritative.**\n**Date:** 2026-07-19\n\n> The full implementation-level specification lives in\n> `notes/adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` (the comprehensive synthesis). This file is the\n> concise parent-bead answer to the three acceptance criteria: *(1) thread-safety approach\n> documented, (2) specific race conditions identified, (3) locking strategy explained or\n> justified as unnecessary.*\n\n---\n\n## 1. One-sentence design\n\n> A single instance-level `asyncio.Lock` on the `TelegramFallback` singleton guards a\n> minimal, **`await`-free** critical section over the first-failure record — because that\n> state is in-memory, per-process, and per-startup. **The lock is defense-in-depth, not a\n> fix for an active bug:** the current synchronous handler is already race-free on a single\n> event loop.\n\n---\n\n## 2. What we are protecting\n\n`TelegramFallback` is a process-wide singleton (`get_telegram_fallback()`, first instantiated\nduring the FastAPI `lifespan` at `src/main.py:152`). It upholds one operational invariant:\n\n> **Exactly one WARNING is logged per process startup** when the bridge first fails; all\n> subsequent failures log at DEBUG.\n\nThe **first-failure record** is four instance attributes (per the data-structure bead\nadc-65l3 and the storage decision adc-2duz — in-memory, per-startup, **no** persistence):\n\n```python\nself._has_logged_first_failure: bool            # the check-then-act flag\nself._failure_count: int                         # read-modify-write counter\nself._last_failure_logged: datetime | None       # updated every failure\nself._first_failure_timestamp: datetime | None   # set-once (to be added — adc-65l3)\n```\n\nAll four are mutated in exactly one place today: `_handle_send_failure`\n(`src/telegram/fallback.py:198`), reachable only through the async `send_message`\n(`fallback.py:84,89,93`). A fifth attribute, `_is_reachable`, is shared mutable state but is\n**deliberately out of scope** for this lock (§5).\n\n---\n\n## 3. The decisive nuance — why the current code is *already* safe (and why we lock anyway)\n\nThis is the single most important point in the design, and it refines what the prior version\nof this file (and the race catalog in adc-4ol5) imply.\n\n**Fact about CPython asyncio:** the event loop runs one task at a time and can only switch\ntasks at a suspension point — an `await` that actually yields. Between suspension points, a\ncoroutine runs without interruption, including any *synchronous* function it calls. A\nsynchronous function with **no `await` inside** is therefore atomic with respect to every\nother coroutine on the loop.\n\nThe current `_handle_send_failure` is exactly that:\n\n```python\ndef _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):   # sync, no `await`\n    self._is_reachable = False\n    self._failure_count += 1\n    now = datetime.now()\n    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n        logger.warning(...)            # stdlib logging — blocking I/O, but does NOT yield\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n        self._last_failure_logged = now\n    else:\n        logger.debug(...)\n```\n\nThere is **no suspension point** anywhere in this body. `datetime.now()` and stdlib\n`logger.*` are synchronous and do not return control to the event loop. So two overlapping\n`send_message` coroutines **cannot interleave** inside `_handle_send_failure` — the\nT0/T1/T2/T3 \"both read False, both warn\" interleaving described in the race catalog cannot\nfire as the code stands.\n\n**Therefore: the race described by the prior docs is *latent*, not *active*.** The invariant\n\"one WARNING per startup\" currently holds because of *incidental* no-await atomicity, not\nbecause of any explicit protection.\n\n**Why we add a lock anyway:**\n\n| Role of the lock | What it buys |\n|---|---|\n| **Defense-in-depth against future `await`s** | The moment a maintainer adds an `await` inside the critical section (async logging handler, a DB persist, an inline notification call), the incidental atomicity evaporates *silently* and the latent race goes live. An explicit lock makes correctness survive that change. |\n| **Explicit, self-documenting contract** | \"No `await` in here\" is an invisible convention. `async with self._first_failure_lock:` makes the critical section's boundaries visible to every reader and reviewer. |\n| **Negligible cost** | Measured ~0.32 µs of isolated overhead (adc-4rh3), on a path that is **dormant today** (see §6). There is no performance reason *not* to take the insurance. |\n\n> **The honest answer to \"is a lock needed?\":** Strictly, no — the synchronous, await-less\n> handler is already atomic on this single-worker, single-loop deployment. We add the lock so\n> that correctness does not depend on a future maintainer knowing that, and so that the\n> notification I/O the design wants to add can be wired in safely.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Race conditions identified\n\nSix races arise when concurrent coroutines enter the failure handler. Cataloged in full in\n`notes/adc-4ol5-race-conditions.md`; the decisive ones:\n\n| # | Race | Pattern | Severity | Status today |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 1 | **Duplicate first-failure WARNINGs** | check-then-act on `_has_logged_first_failure` | **High** — breaks the core invariant | Latent (no `await` to interleave) → **goes live the moment an `await` enters the section** |\n| 2 | **Lost counter updates** | read-modify-write `_failure_count += 1` (LOAD/ADD/STORE) | Medium | Latent for the same reason |\n| 3 | **First-failure timestamp overwrite** | set-once via the racy flag check | Low | Latent |\n| 4 | **Last-failure timestamp lost update** | non-deterministic write ordering | Low | Latent |\n| 5 | **Read-during-write on status check** | `get_bridge_status` reads mid-mutation | Low | Tolerated (stale-but-consistent is fine for monitoring) |\n| 6 | **Reachability toggle** | concurrent success (`_is_reachable = True`) vs failure (`= False`) | Medium | Accepted as benign (single-bytecode writes, last-write-wins) — see §5 |\n\n**The dividing line is the *shape* of the operation, not the field:**\n\n| Shape | Example | Atomic on a single loop? |\n|---|---|---|\n| Single assignment of an immutable | `_has_logged_first_failure = True` | ✅ atomic (one bytecode, and GIL-protected) |\n| Read of a single field | `_failure_count` in `get_bridge_status` | ✅ atomic |\n| **Check-then-act** | `if not flag: flag = True` | ⚠️ atomic *only while there's no `await` between check and act* |\n| **Read-modify-write** | `_failure_count += 1` | ⚠️ atomic *only while there's no `await` between read and write* |\n| Multi-field consistent snapshot | status dict assembled field-by-field | ⚠️ same caveat |\n\nThe GIL makes single bytecodes atomic; it does **not** make compound sequences atomic, and\nasyncio does not make them atomic either — it merely happens not to interrupt them absent a\nsuspension point. That caveat is precisely what the lock removes.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Locking strategy\n\n### 5.1 Decision\n\n> **One instance-level `asyncio.Lock` per `TelegramFallback`, created in `__init__`, scoped to\n> the first-failure record fields only.**\n\n- **All mutations** of the record go through `async with self._first_failure_lock:`.\n- **Reads** (`get_bridge_status`) take **no** lock — single-field atomic reads; monitoring\n  tolerates staleness (Race 5).\n- The critical section contains **no `await`/I/O** — implemented as a pre-locked **plain `def`\n  helper** so I/O is structurally impossible inside it.\n\n### 5.2 Why `asyncio.Lock` and not the alternatives\n\nThe right mechanism is dictated by **where the state lives and which concurrency layer touches\nit.** For this state only the event-loop layer exists (single worker, all-async access paths,\nin-memory per-startup — verified against CLAUDE.md and `src/`):\n\n| Mechanism | Verdict | Why |\n|---|---|---|\n| **`asyncio.Lock` (instance)** | ✅ **Chosen** | Native to async, non-blocking, sub-µs uncontended, groups the multi-field record into one consistent section. |\n| `threading.Lock` | ❌ wrong layer | Blocking acquire stalls the loop; can't be held across `await`. No threaded path touches this state. |\n| Module-level / `app.state` lock | ⚠️ unnecessary | State is owned by the singleton instance; an instance lock is better encapsulated. |\n| Queue-based serialization | ❌ overkill | A background task + queue lifecycle for one boolean flag. |\n\n### 5.3 Correction: \"atomic operations are NOT POSSIBLE\" is false\n\nThe prior version of this file (Option 4) declared atomic operations impossible in Python and\nconcluded `asyncio.Lock` is the *only* viable mechanism. That reasoning is **wrong**, and the\ncorrection matters for future maintainers:\n\n- **GIL-level atomics** exist for single-bytecode operations (§4 table).\n- **SQLite transactions are atomic** — strictly *stronger* than an in-memory lock for\n  persisted state (survive restarts, span workers). `src/session/store.py` already runs\n  `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`, and `aiosqlite` is already a dependency.\n- **File-level atomics** (`os.replace` temp-then-rename) exist for on-disk state.\n\nThe right mental model is **pick the mechanism that matches where the state lives**, not\n\"always reach for `asyncio.Lock`.\" For the first-failure record the *conclusion* (use\n`asyncio.Lock`) is correct — but the *reason* is \"because this state is in-memory,\nper-process, per-startup,\" **not** \"because atomics are impossible.\" Recording the right reason\nprevents a future maintainer from mis-generalizing and blocking a better design elsewhere\n(e.g. a genuine `find_or_create_topic` TOCTOU, which should be fixed with a unique index +\n`INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING … RETURNING`, not a lock).\n\n### 5.4 `_is_reachable` is explicitly out of scope\n\n`_is_reachable` is written from *different* paths (success at `:80`, health-check at `:176,179`,\nfailure at `:207`). **Do not pull it under the first-failure lock:** every write is a single\natomic `STORE_ATTR` with no check-then-act, it has a different lifecycle, and coupling it to a\nfailure-path lock would force the success/health-check paths to acquire a failure lock for no\nreason. Rule of thumb: **one lock per logical state object.**\n\n### 5.5 The structural rules (the parts a reviewer must enforce)\n\n1. **One `asyncio.Lock`** per instance, created in `__init__` (safe — singleton built on the\n   serving loop, constructor does not `await`).\n2. **No `await` inside the critical section.** Enforce by putting the body in a plain `def`\n   helper (`_record_failure_locked`) — a `def` mechanically cannot `await`.\n3. **I/O runs after release**, keyed on a `was_first: bool` captured inside the section.\n4. **Never nest two `async with self._first_failure_lock:`** — `asyncio.Lock` is non-reentrant;\n   re-entry deadlocks. There is no async `RLock`; the pre-locked helper is the fix.\n5. **No lock timeout** — the section is await-free, so a holder can only be preempted between\n   bytecodes for microseconds; a timeout would add a failure mode with no good recovery.\n6. **`get_bridge_status` and all `_is_reachable` writes stay lock-free.**\n\nSketch (full version + diff in `adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` §5–§6):\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n    was_first = False\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n        was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context)  # plain def; no await\n    if was_first:\n        await self._notify_first_failure(error_context)         # I/O outside the lock\n\ndef _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"Caller MUST hold _first_failure_lock. Sync on purpose — no await.\"\"\"\n    ...\n```\n\n---\n\n## 6. Performance (corrected)\n\nMeasured on CPython 3.12 on this box (full benchmark + listing in adc-4rh3; logging raised to\nCRITICAL to isolate lock cost from handler I/O):\n\n| Operation | Measured cost |\n|---|---|\n| Uncontended `async with lock:` (empty body) | **~0.47 µs** |\n| Lock-free status read | **~0.09 µs** |\n| Isolated lock overhead (realistic section, with − without) | **~0.32 µs** |\n| 100 fully-serialized failures | **~60–90 µs total** (count exactly correct; flat to 128 contenders) |\n\n> **Correction to the prior version of this file.** It carried \"~1–2 ms per failure\",\n> \"2–5 ms per request\", and \"100 concurrent failures = 200 ms total\" figures. Measurement\n> shows the lock itself costs ~0.32 µs and 100 serialized failures complete in **~60–90 µs**.\n> The prior figures are **overstated by ~3,000–4,000×** — they conflated lock acquisition cost\n> (sub-µs) with logging-I/O latency (ms, only if a handler does I/O). **Do not propagate them.**\n\nThe decisive argument is grounded in the code, not the microbenchmark: the protected path is\n**dormant today.** `_handle_send_failure` is reachable only via `send_message`, and\n`send_message`/`send_result` have **no live callers** outside `fallback.py` (verified across\n`src/` — `send_exception` and `send_workload_summary` are no-op stubs). The only live touchers\nof the singleton are the lifespan health-check (writes `_is_reachable`, not the record) and the\nread-only status endpoint. **You cannot contend a lock on a code path that isn't called.**\n\nThe one real performance risk is **I/O inside the critical section** (e.g. calling a 10 s-timeout\nnotification `await` inside the lock → every concurrent failing request serializes behind one\n10 s timeout). That is structurally prevented by the plain-`def` helper (§5.5 rule 2).\n\n---\n\n## 7. Edge cases (summary)\n\n| Case | Resolution |\n|---|---|\n| Lock timeout | None added (§5.5 rule 5). If ever added, on-timeout behavior must be \"log + skip the update,\" never a partial write. |\n| Deadlock / self-reentrancy | Prevented: single lock + pre-locked `def` helper; review rule \"never nest two `async with` on the same lock.\" |\n| Cancellation while waiting / holding | `async with` releases on `CancelledError`; body never runs or runs atomically; lock never leaked. |\n| Cancellation during post-lock notification | Only the (best-effort) alert is lost; the record was already written, so the one-WARNING guarantee holds. |\n| Exception inside the section | `async with` releases the lock; read the decision first, write last so an exception is most likely before any field is touched. |\n| Reset vs. in-flight failure | Both `reset_first_failure_state` and `_handle_send_failure` take the same lock → they serialize cleanly; no contradictory state. |\n| Lazy-singleton init | `get_telegram_fallback()` is safe because `__init__` does not `await` — no yield between the `is None` check and the assignment. Would need to move into `lifespan` if `__init` ever awaits. |\n| Multi-worker / restart | In-memory lock does not coordinate across workers or after restart. Acceptable: deployment is single-worker, and the semantic is \"one WARNING **per startup**.\" If it becomes \"one per **deployment**,\" escalate to the SQLite-atomic CAS (§8). |\n\n---\n\n## 8. Upgrade path (when atomics become the right answer)\n\nSwitch the record to the SQLite-atomic pattern (`UPDATE … SET claimed=1, first_failure_at=? WHERE\nid=1 AND claimed=0` + `cur.rowcount == 1` as the compare-and-set; `aiosqlite` already a dep, no\nnew deps) **if any of these becomes true:**\n\n- State must survive restarts (one first-failure per deployment, not per startup).\n- adc is scaled to `uvicorn --workers N`.\n- A `def` route or threadpool path starts touching this state.\n\nThis is a **correctness/durability** escalation (a SQL round-trip is ~0.1–1 ms vs ~0.32 µs for the\nlock), not a performance one. Recorded so it need not be re-derived.\n\n---\n\n## 9. Verification pointers\n\nTests to add alongside the implementation (details in adc-5xuy §10 / adc-4rh3 §11):\n\n- **Concurrent first-failure:** `asyncio.gather` of N `_handle_send_failure` → exactly one\n  WARNING, `failure_count == N`, single `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n- **Counter accuracy:** 100 concurrent calls → `failure_count == 100`.\n- **Structural no-I/O guard:** `assert inspect.iscoroutinefunction(fallback._record_failure_locked)\n  is False` — the load-bearing invariant.\n- **Reset-vs-failure** and **read-doesn't-block** tests.\n\n---\n\n## 10. Corrections logged vs. the prior version of this file\n\n| Prior claim (2026-07-08) | Status | Why |\n|---|---|---|\n| \"Atomic operations NOT POSSIBLE\" (Option 4) | **Wrong** | GIL atomics, SQLite txns, and `os.replace` all exist. Use `asyncio.Lock` because the state is in-memory per-startup, not because atomics are impossible. |\n| \"~2–5 ms per request\", \"100 failures = 200 ms\" | **Overstated ~3,000–4,000×** | Measured ~0.32 µs lock overhead; 100 failures ≈ 60–90 µs. Conflated lock cost with logging-I/O latency. |\n| Current code \"breaks the moment two requests fail in overlapping coroutines\" | **Imprecise** | Overlapping coroutines do **not** interleave inside a synchronous, await-less function on a single event loop. The race is **latent**, held off by incidental no-await atomicity. The lock makes the property robust rather than incidental. |\n| `asyncio.Lock` recommended (Option 1) | **Correct** | Conclusion stands; reasoning refined. |\n| Race catalog (duplicate WARNING, lost counter, timestamp overwrite) | **Correct** | Unchanged; reframed as latent. |\n\n---\n\n## References\n\n- **Comprehensive implementation spec:** `notes/adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` (adc-5xuy) — the\n  deep dive; concrete diff, reviewer checklist, cheat sheet.\n- **Race catalog:** `notes/adc-4ol5-race-conditions.md` (adc-4ol5).\n- **Mechanism research:** `notes/adc-4783.md` (adc-4783) — `asyncio.Lock` behavior, GIL atomicity,\n  SQLite-atomic CAS pattern, correction to \"atomics impossible.\"\n- **Strategy decision:** `notes/adc-41u0.md` (adc-41u0).\n- **Performance analysis:** `notes/adc-4rh3.md` (adc-4rh3) — measured costs; benchmark at\n  `~/scratch/adc-4rh3-lock-bench.py`.\n- **Data structure / storage (dependencies):** `notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md`\n  (adc-65l3), `notes/adc-2duz-state-storage-design.md` (adc-2duz) — storage decision is\n  in-memory, per-startup, no persistence.\n- **Current code:** `src/telegram/fallback.py` — `_handle_send_failure` at `:198`, call sites at\n  `:84,89,93`, `__init__` at `:36`; singleton created in `lifespan` at `src/main.py:152`; status\n  read at `src/main.py:1474`. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_681510b6698b4458b30ef701","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# First-Failure State Data Structure\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\tThis document defines the data structure for tracking first-failure state after Telegram send startup in aide-de-camp. The state tracks whether the first failure after application startup has been logged, preventing log spam while ensuring visibility of bridge connectivity issues.\n6\t\n7\t## State Structure Definition\n8\t\n9\t### Location\n10\tThe state lives as instance variables in the `TelegramFallback` class (`src/telegram/fallback.py`), accessed via the singleton `get_telegram_fallback()`.\n11\t\n12\t### Data Structure\n13\t\n14\t```python\n15\tfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\n16\tfrom datetime import datetime\n17\tfrom typing import Optional\n18\timport asyncio\n19\t\n20\t@dataclass\n21\tclass FirstFailureState:\n22\t    \"\"\"\n23\t    Tracks the first-failure detection state for Telegram sends.\n24\t    \n25\t    This state ensures that only ONE WARNING-level log is emitted per\n26\t    application startup when the Telegram bridge becomes unreachable,\n27\t    while all subsequent failures are logged at DEBUG level to avoid spam.\n28\t    \"\"\"\n29\t    \n30\t    # Primary first-failure flag\n31\t    has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n32\t    \"\"\"\n33\t    Whether the first failure after startup has been logged.\n34\t    \n35\t    - False: No failures detected yet, or state has been reset\n36\t    - True: First failure has been logged at WARNING level\n37\t    \n38\t    This is the primary flag that controls logging behavior:\n39\t    - When False: Next failure logs at WARNING level\n40\t    - When True: All subsequent failures log at DEBUG level\n41\t    \n42\t    Reset scenarios:\n43\t    - Application restart (automatic via singleton lifecycle)\n44\t    - Manual reset via reset_first_failure_state() method (future)\n45\t    - Recovery-based reset after N consecutive successes (future)\n46\t    \"\"\"\n47\t    \n48\t    # Failure tracking\n49\t    failure_count: int = 0\n50\t    \"\"\"\n51\t    Total number of send failures since application startup.\n52\t    \n53\t    Increments on every send_message() failure.\n54\t    Used for:\n55\t    - Status reporting (get_bridge_status())\n56\t    - Monitoring and metrics\n57\t    - Potential threshold-based escalation (future)\n58\t    \n59\t    Does NOT reset on first-failure detection — only on application restart\n60\t    or explicit reset.\n61\t    \"\"\"\n62\t    \n63\t    # Timestamps\n64\t    first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n65\t    \"\"\"\n66\t    Timestamp of the very first failure after application startup.\n67\t    \n68\t    Set once when the first failure occurs and never updated again\n69\t    (until application restart or explicit reset).\n70\t    \n71\t    Used for:\n72\t    - Diagnostics: \"Bridge has been down since X\"\n73\t    - Metrics: Time-to-detection of bridge issues\n74\t    - Correlation with other events\n75\t    \n76\t    Reset scenarios:\n77\t    - Application restart (becomes None again)\n78\t    - Manual reset (set back to None)\n79\t    \"\"\"\n80\t    \n81\t    last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n82\t    \"\"\"\n83\t    Timestamp of the most recent failure.\n84\t    \n85\t    Updated on every failure (not just the first).\n86\t    \n87\t    Used for:\n88\t    - Status reporting: \"Last failure X seconds ago\"\n89\t    - Health checks: Detect if failures are ongoing or intermittent\n90\t    - Cooldown enforcement: Avoid rapid retry loops\n91\t    \n92\t    Reset scenarios:\n93\t    - Never resets to None during application lifetime\n94\t    - Always updates to the most recent failure time\n95\t    \"\"\"\n96\t    \n97\t    # Thread safety\n98\t    _lock: asyncio.Lock = field(default_factory=asyncio.Lock)\n99\t    \"\"\"\n100\t    Asyncio lock for thread-safe state mutations.\n101\t    \n102\t    Protects the check-and-set sequence in _handle_send_failure()\n103\t    to prevent race conditions when multiple concurrent requests fail.\n104\t    \n105\t    All state mutations MUST acquire this lock first:\n106\t    - has_logged_first_failure = True\n107\t    - failure_count += 1\n108\t    - first_failure_timestamp assignment\n109\t    - last_failure_timestamp assignment\n110\t    \n111\t    Read operations (get_bridge_status()) do NOT require the lock\n112\t    as they read immutable values or don't require consistency.\n113\t    \"\"\"\n114\t```\n115\t\n116\t## Integration with TelegramFallback Class\n117\t\n118\t### Instance Initialization\n119\t\n120\t```python\n121\tclass TelegramFallback:\n122\t    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n123\t        # ... existing initialization ...\n124\t        \n125\t        # First-failure tracking state (flat instance variables)\n126\t        self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n127\t        self._failure_count: int = 0\n128\t        self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n129\t        self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n130\t        self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n131\t```\n132\t\n133\t### State Mutation Pattern (Thread-Safe)\n134\t\n135\t```python\n136\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n137\t    \"\"\"\n138\t    Handle a send failure with thread-safe first-failure detection.\n139\t    \n140\t    All state mutations occur under lock protection to prevent\n141\t    race conditions from concurrent async requests.\n142\t    \"\"\"\n143\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Serialize access\n144\t        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n145\t            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n146\t            logger.warning(\n147\t                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n148\t                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n149\t                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n150\t            )\n151\t            self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n152\t            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n153\t            self._failure_count = 1\n154\t            self._last_failure_logged = datetime.now()\n155\t        else:\n156\t            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n157\t            logger.debug(\n158\t                f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count + 1} \"\n159\t                f\"at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n160\t                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}.\"\n161\t            )\n162\t            self._failure_count += 1\n163\t            self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n164\t```\n165\t\n166\t### Read Access Pattern (No Lock Required)\n167\t\n168\t```python\n169\tdef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n170\t    \"\"\"\n171\t    Get the current bridge status.\n172\t    \n173\t    Read operations do NOT require the lock as they:\n174\t    - Read immutable values (bool, int)\n175\t    - Don't require cross-field consistency\n176\t    - Are used for status/reporting, not decision-making\n177\t    \"\"\"\n178\t    return {\n179\t        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n180\t        \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n181\t        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n182\t        \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,\n183\t        \"first_failure_timestamp\": self._first_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n184\t            if self._first_failure_timestamp else None,\n185\t        \"last_failure_timestamp\": self._last_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n186\t            if self._last_failure_timestamp else None,\n187\t    }\n188\t```\n189\t\n190\t## Reset Scenarios\n191\t\n192\t### 1. Automatic Reset on Application Restart\n193\t\n194\t**Mechanism:** Process exit → `_telegram_fallback` goes out of scope → new process → fresh singleton → default state\n195\t\n196\t**What resets:** All fields return to defaults:\n197\t- `has_logged_first_failure = False`\n198\t- `failure_count = 0`\n199\t- `first_failure_timestamp = None`\n200\t- `last_failure_timestamp = None`\n201\t\n202\t**Why:** First-failure detection is a per-startup diagnostic. Each restart gets a fresh window to detect \"is the bridge down right now?\"\n203\t\n204\t**Result:** ✅ **Automatic** — No manual intervention required\n205\t\n206\t### 2. Manual Reset (Future Feature)\n207\t\n208\t**Mechanism:** Call a reset method on the singleton instance\n209\t\n210\t```python\n211\tasync def reset_first_failure_state(self) -> None:\n212\t    \"\"\"\n213\t    Manually reset the first-failure tracking state.\n214\t    \n215\t    Use cases:\n216\t    - After bridge recovery confirmation\n217\t    - After manual bridge fix\n218\t    - Testing scenarios\n219\t    \n220\t    Does NOT reset failure_count (keeps running total).\n221\t    \"\"\"\n222\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n223\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n224\t        self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n225\t        # Keep failure_count and last_failure_timestamp for diagnostics\n226\t```\n227\t\n228\t**What resets:**\n229\t- `has_logged_first_failure = False`\n230\t- `first_failure_timestamp = None`\n231\t\n232\t**What does NOT reset:**\n233\t- `failure_count` (keeps running total for diagnostics)\n234\t- `last_failure_timestamp` (most recent failure still relevant)\n235\t\n236\t**Result:** ✅ **Intentional** — Explicit action by operator or code\n237\t\n238\t### 3. Recovery-Based Reset (Future Feature)\n239\t\n240\t**Mechanism:** After N consecutive successful sends, reset the flag\n241\t\n242\t```python\n243\tasync def _handle_send_success(self):\n244\t    \"\"\"Handle a successful send (future method).\"\"\"\n245\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n246\t        if self._consecutive_success_count >= self._recovery_threshold:\n247\t            self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n248\t            self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n249\t            self._consecutive_success_count = 0\n250\t```\n251\t\n252\t**What resets:** Same as manual reset\n253\t\n254\t**When:** After configurable number of consecutive successes (e.g., 5)\n255\t\n256\t**Why:** If the bridge recovers, allow a new first-failure WARNING on next degradation without requiring restart\n257\t\n258\t**Result:** ✅ **Conditional** — Automatic but based on recovery detection\n259\t\n260\t## State Lifecycle Diagram\n261\t\n262\t```\n263\tApplication Startup\n264\t    │\n265\t    ├─► get_telegram_fallback() called\n266\t    │   └─► TelegramFallback() instance created\n267\t    │       └─► All state fields at defaults\n268\t    │           ├─► has_logged_first_failure = False\n269\t    │           ├─► failure_count = 0\n270\t    │           ├─► first_failure_timestamp = None\n271\t    │           └─► last_failure_timestamp = None\n272\t    │\n273\t    ├─► First send_message() failure occurs\n274\t    │   └─► _handle_send_failure() under lock\n275\t    │       ├─► Check: has_logged_first_failure == False?\n276\t    │       ├─► YES → Log WARNING, set flag\n277\t    │       │   ├─► has_logged_first_failure = True\n278\t    │       │   ├─► first_failure_timestamp = now\n279\t    │       │   ├─► failure_count = 1\n280\t    │       │   └─► last_failure_timestamp = now\n281\t    │       │\n282\t    │       └─► User sees WARNING in logs\n283\t    │\n284\t    ├─► Subsequent failures\n285\t    │   └─► _handle_send_failure() under lock\n286\t    │       ├─► Check: has_logged_first_failure == True?\n287\t    │       ├─► YES → Log DEBUG only, increment counter\n288\t    │       │   ├─► failure_count += 1\n289\t    │       │   └─► last_failure_timestamp = now\n290\t    │       │\n291\t    │       └─► User sees DEBUG logs (no WARNING spam)\n292\t    │\n293\t    ├─► Application restart (process exit)\n294\t    │   └─► New process → fresh singleton → all defaults\n295\t    │       └─► Next failure logs WARNING again\n296\t    │\n297\t    └─► Manual reset (future)\n298\t        └─► reset_first_failure_state() called\n299\t            └─► Flag resets → next failure logs WARNING\n300\t```\n301\t\n302\t## Usage Examples\n303\t\n304\t### Example 1: Normal Operation (Bridge Up)\n305\t\n306\t```python\n307\t# Startup: all fields at defaults\n308\tfallback = get_telegram_fallback()\n309\tassert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False\n310\tassert fallback._failure_count == 0\n311\t\n312\t# Send succeeds → no state changes\n313\tawait fallback.send_message(chat_id, \"Hello\")\n314\tassert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False  # unchanged\n315\tassert fallback._failure_count == 0  # unchanged\n316\t```\n317\t\n318\t### Example 2: Bridge Failure Scenario\n319\t\n320\t```python\n321\t# First failure occurs\n322\tawait fallback.send_message(chat_id, \"Test\")\n323\t# → Logs WARNING (first failure detected)\n324\t# → State updated under lock:\n325\t#    has_logged_first_failure = True\n326\t#    failure_count = 1\n327\t#    first_failure_timestamp = 2026-07-02T14:30:00Z\n328\t#    last_failure_timestamp = 2026-07-02T14:30:00Z\n329\t\n330\t# Subsequent failures (10 more concurrent requests)\n331\tawait asyncio.gather(*[fallback.send_message(chat_id, f\"msg{i}\") for i in range(10)])\n332\t# → Logs 10 DEBUG messages (no WARNING spam)\n333\t# → State updated:\n334\t#    failure_count = 11\n335\t#    last_failure_timestamp = 2026-07-02T14:31:45Z\n336\t#    first_failure_timestamp = unchanged (still 14:30:00Z)\n337\t\n338\t# Check status\n339\tstatus = fallback.get_bridge_status()\n340\tassert status[\"failure_count\"] == 11\n341\tassert status[\"has_logged_first_failure\"] == True\n342\tassert status[\"first_failure_timestamp\"] == \"2026-07-02T14:30:00+00:00\"\n343\t```\n344\t\n345\t### Example 3: Application Restart\n346\t\n347\t```python\n348\t# Before restart: failure_count = 11, flag = True\n349\t# Application restarts (process exit, new process)\n350\t# After restart: fresh singleton\n351\tfallback = get_telegram_fallback()\n352\tassert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False  # Reset!\n353\tassert fallback._failure_count = 0  # Reset!\n354\tassert fallback._first_failure_timestamp is None  # Reset!\n355\t\n356\t# Next failure logs WARNING again (new first-failure detection)\n357\tawait fallback.send_message(chat_id, \"Test after restart\")\n358\t# → Logs WARNING (first failure of new startup)\n359\t```\n360\t\n361\t## Type Safety\n362\t\n363\tAll fields use proper type hints for static analysis:\n364\t\n365\t```python\n366\tfrom typing import Optional\n367\t\n368\thas_logged_first_failure: bool              # Always True or False\n369\tfailure_count: int                          # Always non-negative integer\n370\tfirst_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]  # datetime or None\n371\tlast_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]  # datetime or None\n372\t_first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock          # Always a Lock instance\n373\t```\n374\t\n375\t## Future Extensions\n376\t\n377\tThe state structure is designed to support future enhancements:\n378\t\n379\t### 1. Error Type Tracking\n380\t\n381\t```python\n382\t# Add field to track error categories\n383\tfirst_failure_error_type: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"timeout\", \"connection_refused\", \"5xx_error\"\n384\t```\n385\t\n386\t### 2. Channel-Specific Tracking (Multi-Bridge)\n387\t\n388\t```python\n389\t# Add field to track which channel failed\n390\tfirst_failure_channel: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"telegram-claude-bridge\", \"fallback-bridge\"\n391\t```\n392\t\n393\t### 3. Recovery Threshold\n394\t\n395\t```python\n396\t# Add fields for recovery-based reset\n397\tconsecutive_success_count: int = 0\n398\trecovery_threshold: int = 5  # Reset after 5 consecutive successes\n399\t```\n400\t\n401\t## Summary\n402\t\n403\t**State Structure:**\n404\t- `has_logged_first_failure: bool` — Primary flag (False → True, once per startup)\n405\t- `failure_count: int` — Total failures since startup\n406\t- `first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]` — Timestamp of first failure\n407\t- `last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]` — Timestamp of most recent failure\n408\t- `_first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock` — Thread-safety for mutations\n409\t\n410\t**Storage:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` singleton\n411\t\n412\t**Reset Scenarios:**\n413\t- ✅ Application restart (automatic, all fields)\n414\t- ✅ Manual reset (future, selective fields)\n415\t- ✅ Recovery-based reset (future, selective fields)\n416\t\n417\t**Thread-Safety:** All mutations protected by `asyncio.Lock`\n418\t\n419\t**Type Safety:** All fields properly typed for static analysis\n420\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"56d9ffc9-24ed-4173-9257-9d7110998b7f","uuid":"29a88863-c921-49d8-94a0-761cdd8dec48","timestamp":"2026-07-19T22:43:38.572Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md","content":"# First-Failure State Data Structure\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document defines the data structure for tracking first-failure state after Telegram send startup in aide-de-camp. The state tracks whether the first failure after application startup has been logged, preventing log spam while ensuring visibility of bridge connectivity issues.\n\n## State Structure Definition\n\n### Location\nThe state lives as instance variables in the `TelegramFallback` class (`src/telegram/fallback.py`), accessed via the singleton `get_telegram_fallback()`.\n\n### Data Structure\n\n```python\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\nfrom datetime import datetime\nfrom typing import Optional\nimport asyncio\n\n@dataclass\nclass FirstFailureState:\n    \"\"\"\n    Tracks the first-failure detection state for Telegram sends.\n    \n    This state ensures that only ONE WARNING-level log is emitted per\n    application startup when the Telegram bridge becomes unreachable,\n    while all subsequent failures are logged at DEBUG level to avoid spam.\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # Primary first-failure flag\n    has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n    \"\"\"\n    Whether the first failure after startup has been logged.\n    \n    - False: No failures detected yet, or state has been reset\n    - True: First failure has been logged at WARNING level\n    \n    This is the primary flag that controls logging behavior:\n    - When False: Next failure logs at WARNING level\n    - When True: All subsequent failures log at DEBUG level\n    \n    Reset scenarios:\n    - Application restart (automatic via singleton lifecycle)\n    - Manual reset via reset_first_failure_state() method (future)\n    - Recovery-based reset after N consecutive successes (future)\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # Failure tracking\n    failure_count: int = 0\n    \"\"\"\n    Total number of send failures since application startup.\n    \n    Increments on every send_message() failure.\n    Used for:\n    - Status reporting (get_bridge_status())\n    - Monitoring and metrics\n    - Potential threshold-based escalation (future)\n    \n    Does NOT reset on first-failure detection — only on application restart\n    or explicit reset.\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # Timestamps\n    first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n    \"\"\"\n    Timestamp of the very first failure after application startup.\n    \n    Set once when the first failure occurs and never updated again\n    (until application restart or explicit reset).\n    \n    Used for:\n    - Diagnostics: \"Bridge has been down since X\"\n    - Metrics: Time-to-detection of bridge issues\n    - Correlation with other events\n    \n    Reset scenarios:\n    - Application restart (becomes None again)\n    - Manual reset (set back to None)\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n    \"\"\"\n    Timestamp of the most recent failure.\n    \n    Updated on every failure (not just the first).\n    \n    Used for:\n    - Status reporting: \"Last failure X seconds ago\"\n    - Health checks: Detect if failures are ongoing or intermittent\n    - Cooldown enforcement: Avoid rapid retry loops\n    \n    Reset scenarios:\n    - Never resets to None during application lifetime\n    - Always updates to the most recent failure time\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # Thread safety\n    _lock: asyncio.Lock = field(default_factory=asyncio.Lock)\n    \"\"\"\n    Asyncio lock for thread-safe state mutations.\n    \n    Protects the check-and-set sequence in _handle_send_failure()\n    to prevent race conditions when multiple concurrent requests fail.\n    \n    All state mutations MUST acquire this lock first:\n    - has_logged_first_failure = True\n    - failure_count += 1\n    - first_failure_timestamp assignment\n    - last_failure_timestamp assignment\n    \n    Read operations (get_bridge_status()) do NOT require the lock\n    as they read immutable values or don't require consistency.\n    \"\"\"\n```\n\n## Integration with TelegramFallback Class\n\n### Instance Initialization\n\n```python\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        # ... existing initialization ...\n        \n        # First-failure tracking state (flat instance variables)\n        self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n        self._failure_count: int = 0\n        self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n        self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n        self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n```\n\n### State Mutation Pattern (Thread-Safe)\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    \"\"\"\n    Handle a send failure with thread-safe first-failure detection.\n    \n    All state mutations occur under lock protection to prevent\n    race conditions from concurrent async requests.\n    \"\"\"\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Serialize access\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n            logger.warning(\n                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n            )\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n            self._failure_count = 1\n            self._last_failure_logged = datetime.now()\n        else:\n            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n            logger.debug(\n                f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count + 1} \"\n                f\"at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}.\"\n            )\n            self._failure_count += 1\n            self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n```\n\n### Read Access Pattern (No Lock Required)\n\n```python\ndef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n    \"\"\"\n    Get the current bridge status.\n    \n    Read operations do NOT require the lock as they:\n    - Read immutable values (bool, int)\n    - Don't require cross-field consistency\n    - Are used for status/reporting, not decision-making\n    \"\"\"\n    return {\n        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n        \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n        \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,\n        \"first_failure_timestamp\": self._first_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n            if self._first_failure_timestamp else None,\n        \"last_failure_timestamp\": self._last_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n            if self._last_failure_timestamp else None,\n    }\n```\n\n## Reset Scenarios\n\n### 1. Automatic Reset on Application Restart\n\n**Mechanism:** Process exit → `_telegram_fallback` goes out of scope → new process → fresh singleton → default state\n\n**What resets:** All fields return to defaults:\n- `has_logged_first_failure = False`\n- `failure_count = 0`\n- `first_failure_timestamp = None`\n- `last_failure_timestamp = None`\n\n**Why:** First-failure detection is a per-startup diagnostic. Each restart gets a fresh window to detect \"is the bridge down right now?\"\n\n**Result:** ✅ **Automatic** — No manual intervention required\n\n### 2. Manual Reset (Future Feature)\n\n**Mechanism:** Call a reset method on the singleton instance\n\n```python\nasync def reset_first_failure_state(self) -> None:\n    \"\"\"\n    Manually reset the first-failure tracking state.\n    \n    Use cases:\n    - After bridge recovery confirmation\n    - After manual bridge fix\n    - Testing scenarios\n    \n    Does NOT reset failure_count (keeps running total).\n    \"\"\"\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n        # Keep failure_count and last_failure_timestamp for diagnostics\n```\n\n**What resets:**\n- `has_logged_first_failure = False`\n- `first_failure_timestamp = None`\n\n**What does NOT reset:**\n- `failure_count` (keeps running total for diagnostics)\n- `last_failure_timestamp` (most recent failure still relevant)\n\n**Result:** ✅ **Intentional** — Explicit action by operator or code\n\n### 3. Recovery-Based Reset (Future Feature)\n\n**Mechanism:** After N consecutive successful sends, reset the flag\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_success(self):\n    \"\"\"Handle a successful send (future method).\"\"\"\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n        if self._consecutive_success_count >= self._recovery_threshold:\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n            self._consecutive_success_count = 0\n```\n\n**What resets:** Same as manual reset\n\n**When:** After configurable number of consecutive successes (e.g., 5)\n\n**Why:** If the bridge recovers, allow a new first-failure WARNING on next degradation without requiring restart\n\n**Result:** ✅ **Conditional** — Automatic but based on recovery detection\n\n## State Lifecycle Diagram\n\n```\nApplication Startup\n    │\n    ├─► get_telegram_fallback() called\n    │   └─► TelegramFallback() instance created\n    │       └─► All state fields at defaults\n    │           ├─► has_logged_first_failure = False\n    │           ├─► failure_count = 0\n    │           ├─► first_failure_timestamp = None\n    │           └─► last_failure_timestamp = None\n    │\n    ├─► First send_message() failure occurs\n    │   └─► _handle_send_failure() under lock\n    │       ├─► Check: has_logged_first_failure == False?\n    │       ├─► YES → Log WARNING, set flag\n    │       │   ├─► has_logged_first_failure = True\n    │       │   ├─► first_failure_timestamp = now\n    │       │   ├─► failure_count = 1\n    │       │   └─► last_failure_timestamp = now\n    │       │\n    │       └─► User sees WARNING in logs\n    │\n    ├─► Subsequent failures\n    │   └─► _handle_send_failure() under lock\n    │       ├─► Check: has_logged_first_failure == True?\n    │       ├─► YES → Log DEBUG only, increment counter\n    │       │   ├─► failure_count += 1\n    │       │   └─► last_failure_timestamp = now\n    │       │\n    │       └─► User sees DEBUG logs (no WARNING spam)\n    │\n    ├─► Application restart (process exit)\n    │   └─► New process → fresh singleton → all defaults\n    │       └─► Next failure logs WARNING again\n    │\n    └─► Manual reset (future)\n        └─► reset_first_failure_state() called\n            └─► Flag resets → next failure logs WARNING\n```\n\n## Usage Examples\n\n### Example 1: Normal Operation (Bridge Up)\n\n```python\n# Startup: all fields at defaults\nfallback = get_telegram_fallback()\nassert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False\nassert fallback._failure_count == 0\n\n# Send succeeds → no state changes\nawait fallback.send_message(chat_id, \"Hello\")\nassert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False  # unchanged\nassert fallback._failure_count == 0  # unchanged\n```\n\n### Example 2: Bridge Failure Scenario\n\n```python\n# First failure occurs\nawait fallback.send_message(chat_id, \"Test\")\n# → Logs WARNING (first failure detected)\n# → State updated under lock:\n#    has_logged_first_failure = True\n#    failure_count = 1\n#    first_failure_timestamp = 2026-07-02T14:30:00Z\n#    last_failure_timestamp = 2026-07-02T14:30:00Z\n\n# Subsequent failures (10 more concurrent requests)\nawait asyncio.gather(*[fallback.send_message(chat_id, f\"msg{i}\") for i in range(10)])\n# → Logs 10 DEBUG messages (no WARNING spam)\n# → State updated:\n#    failure_count = 11\n#    last_failure_timestamp = 2026-07-02T14:31:45Z\n#    first_failure_timestamp = unchanged (still 14:30:00Z)\n\n# Check status\nstatus = fallback.get_bridge_status()\nassert status[\"failure_count\"] == 11\nassert status[\"has_logged_first_failure\"] == True\nassert status[\"first_failure_timestamp\"] == \"2026-07-02T14:30:00+00:00\"\n```\n\n### Example 3: Application Restart\n\n```python\n# Before restart: failure_count = 11, flag = True\n# Application restarts (process exit, new process)\n# After restart: fresh singleton\nfallback = get_telegram_fallback()\nassert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False  # Reset!\nassert fallback._failure_count = 0  # Reset!\nassert fallback._first_failure_timestamp is None  # Reset!\n\n# Next failure logs WARNING again (new first-failure detection)\nawait fallback.send_message(chat_id, \"Test after restart\")\n# → Logs WARNING (first failure of new startup)\n```\n\n## Type Safety\n\nAll fields use proper type hints for static analysis:\n\n```python\nfrom typing import Optional\n\nhas_logged_first_failure: bool              # Always True or False\nfailure_count: int                          # Always non-negative integer\nfirst_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]  # datetime or None\nlast_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]  # datetime or None\n_first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock          # Always a Lock instance\n```\n\n## Future Extensions\n\nThe state structure is designed to support future enhancements:\n\n### 1. Error Type Tracking\n\n```python\n# Add field to track error categories\nfirst_failure_error_type: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"timeout\", \"connection_refused\", \"5xx_error\"\n```\n\n### 2. Channel-Specific Tracking (Multi-Bridge)\n\n```python\n# Add field to track which channel failed\nfirst_failure_channel: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"telegram-claude-bridge\", \"fallback-bridge\"\n```\n\n### 3. Recovery Threshold\n\n```python\n# Add fields for recovery-based reset\nconsecutive_success_count: int = 0\nrecovery_threshold: int = 5  # Reset after 5 consecutive successes\n```\n\n## Summary\n\n**State Structure:**\n- `has_logged_first_failure: bool` — Primary flag (False → True, once per startup)\n- `failure_count: int` — Total failures since startup\n- `first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]` — Timestamp of first failure\n- `last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]` — Timestamp of most recent failure\n- `_first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock` — Thread-safety for mutations\n\n**Storage:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` singleton\n\n**Reset Scenarios:**\n- ✅ Application restart (automatic, all fields)\n- ✅ Manual reset (future, selective fields)\n- ✅ Recovery-based reset (future, selective fields)\n\n**Thread-Safety:** All mutations protected by `asyncio.Lock`\n\n**Type Safety:** All fields properly typed for static analysis\n","numLines":420,"startLine":1,"totalLines":420}}}
