{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_1d89ea3993e94fd99b829415","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# First-Failure State Storage Design\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\tThis document evaluates storage options for the first-failure tracking state in the async FastAPI application and documents the recommended approach with rationale.\n6\t\n7\t## Design Requirements\n8\t\n9\t### Functional Requirements\n10\t1. State must track first-failure occurrence across application lifetime\n11\t2. State must survive from application startup until shutdown\n12\t3. Only ONE first-failure event should be detected per startup sequence\n13\t4. State must be accessible to all `send_message()` calls\n14\t\n15\t### Non-Functional Requirements\n16\t1. **Thread-safety**: Multiple concurrent async requests must not corrupt state\n17\t2. **Performance**: Minimal overhead on the hot path (Telegram sends)\n18\t3. **Lifecycle clarity**: Clear initialization and reset behavior\n19\t4. **Simplicity**: Easy to understand, test, and maintain\n20\t\n21\t## Storage Options Evaluation\n22\t\n23\t### Option 1: Module-Level Variable\n24\t\n25\t**Approach:**\n26\t```python\n27\t# In src/telegram/fallback.py\n28\t_first_failure_state = {\n29\t    \"has_logged_first_failure\": False,\n30\t    \"failure_count\": 0,\n31\t    \"first_failure_timestamp\": None,\n32\t    \"last_failure_timestamp\": None,\n33\t}\n34\t```\n35\t\n36\t**Pros:**\n37\t- Simple to understand\n38\t- Accessible from anywhere in the module\n39\t- No instance overhead\n40\t\n41\t**Cons:**\n42\t- ❌ **Not thread-safe**: Concurrent async coroutines can race on check-and-set\n43\t- ❌ **Hard to test**: Global state pollutes tests; requires manual reset\n44\t- ❌ **No encapsulation**: Any code can mutate state directly\n45\t- ❌ **No lock integration**: Would need a separate global lock object\n46\t\n47\t**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Not thread-safe in async context\n48\t\n49\t---\n50\t\n51\t### Option 2: Class Attribute (Shared State)\n52\t\n53\t**Approach:**\n54\t```python\n55\tclass TelegramFallback:\n56\t    # Class attribute - shared across ALL instances\n57\t    _has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n58\t    _first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = None\n59\t    \n60\t    @classmethod\n61\t    async def get_class_lock(cls):\n62\t        if cls._first_failure_lock is None:\n63\t            cls._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n64\t        return cls._first_failure_lock\n65\t```\n66\t\n67\t**Pros:**\n68\t- State shared across all instances (if multiple were created)\n69\t- Class-level organization\n70\t\n71\t**Cons:**\n72\t- ❌ **Unnecessary complexity**: `TelegramFallback` is a singleton anyway\n73\t- ❌ **Lock initialization timing**: Class attrs init at import time, before event loop\n74\t- ❌ **Testing complexity**: Hard to reset between tests\n75\t- ❌ **Surprising behavior**: Shared state across instances is non-obvious\n76\t\n77\t**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Adds complexity without benefit\n78\t\n79\t---\n80\t\n81\t### Option 3: Instance Variable (Per-Instance State)\n82\t\n83\t**Approach:**\n84\t```python\n85\tclass TelegramFallback:\n86\t    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n87\t        # Instance variables\n88\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n89\t        self._failure_count = 0\n90\t        self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n91\t        self._last_failure_timestamp = None\n92\t        self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n93\t```\n94\t\n95\t**Pros:**\n96\t- ✅ **Thread-safe with lock**: `asyncio.Lock` serializes concurrent access\n97\t- ✅ **Clear lifecycle**: Initializes when instance creates, dies when instance destroyed\n98\t- ✅ **Singleton pattern**: `get_telegram_fallback()` ensures one instance per app\n99\t- ✅ **Encapsulated**: State lives inside the class that uses it\n100\t- ✅ **Testable**: Each test can create a fresh instance\n101\t- ✅ **Natural reset**: Application restart = new instance = reset state\n102\t\n103\t**Cons:**\n104\t- None significant for this use case\n105\t\n106\t**Verdict:** ✅ **RECOMMENDED** - Matches all requirements\n107\t\n108\t---\n109\t\n110\t### Option 4: Request Context (Per-Request State)\n111\t\n112\t**Approach:**\n113\t```python\n114\tfrom starlette.requests import Request\n115\t\n116\tasync def send_message(request: Request, chat_id, message):\n117\t    # Store in request.state\n118\t    if not hasattr(request.state, \"first_failure_logged\"):\n119\t        request.state.first_failure_logged = True\n120\t        logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n121\t```\n122\t\n123\t**Pros:**\n124\t- Natural isolation per request\n125\t\n126\t**Cons:**\n127\t- ❌ **Wrong lifecycle**: Request-scoped means state resets EVERY request\n128\t- ❌ **Violates requirement**: Need ONE first-failure per startup, not per request\n129\t- ❌ **Impossible**: Would need to persist across requests (defeats the purpose)\n130\t\n131\t**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Wrong lifecycle for this requirement\n132\t\n133\t---\n134\t\n135\t### Option 5: Database Persistence\n136\t\n137\t**Approach:**\n138\t```python\n139\t# Store in SQLite or PostgreSQL\n140\tCREATE TABLE first_failure_state (\n141\t    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,\n142\t    has_logged_first_failure BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,\n143\t    failure_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,\n144\t    first_failure_timestamp TIMESTAMP,\n145\t    last_failure_timestamp TIMESTAMP\n146\t);\n147\t```\n148\t\n149\t**Pros:**\n150\t- Survives application restarts\n151\t- Queryable for monitoring\n152\t\n153\t**Cons:**\n154\t- ❌ **Wrong lifecycle**: First-failure should RESET on restart, not persist\n155\t- ❌ **Performance overhead**: Database write on every failure (hot path)\n156\t- ❌ **Overkill**: No need for persistence; state is transient by design\n157\t- ❌ **Complexity**: Adds database dependency for simple boolean flag\n158\t\n159\t**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Adds complexity for wrong lifecycle\n160\t\n161\t---\n162\t\n163\t## Recommended Design: Instance Variable with Singleton\n164\t\n165\t### Storage Location\n166\t\n167\t**Primary storage:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` class\n168\t\n169\t```python\n170\t# src/telegram/fallback.py\n171\tclass TelegramFallback:\n172\t    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n173\t        # ... existing initialization ...\n174\t        \n175\t        # First-failure tracking state (instance variables)\n176\t        self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n177\t        self._failure_count: int = 0\n178\t        self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n179\t        self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n180\t        \n181\t        # Thread-safety: async lock for concurrent access\n182\t        self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n183\t```\n184\t\n185\t### Global Access via Singleton\n186\t\n187\tThe application uses a module-level singleton pattern:\n188\t\n189\t```python\n190\t# Global instance (module-level)\n191\t_telegram_fallback: Optional[TelegramFallback] = None\n192\t\n193\tdef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n194\t    \"\"\"Get or create the global Telegram fallback instance.\"\"\"\n195\t    global _telegram_fallback\n196\t    if _telegram_fallback is None:\n197\t        _telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()\n198\t    return _telegram_fallback\n199\t```\n200\t\n201\t**Why this works:**\n202\t1. **First call**: Creates the singleton instance, initializes state\n203\t2. **Subsequent calls**: Returns the same instance with preserved state\n204\t3. **Application lifetime**: One instance lives for entire app runtime\n205\t4. **Application restart**: Process exit → `_telegram_fallback = None` → fresh state on next startup\n206\t\n207\t### Initialization Timing\n208\t\n209\t| Event | What Happens | State Value |\n210\t|-------|-------------|-------------|\n211\t| **Application startup** | `get_telegram_fallback()` called for first time | All fields at defaults |\n212\t| **Module import** | `TelegramFallback` class defined | No instance yet |\n213\t| **First use** | `send_message()` → `get_telegram_fallback()` → `__init__()` | Instance created, state initialized |\n214\t| **First failure** | `_handle_send_failure()` under lock | `_has_logged_first_failure = True` |\n215\t| **Application restart** | Process exits, new process starts | Fresh instance = fresh state |\n216\t\n217\t### Reset Behavior\n218\t\n219\t| Scenario | Mechanism | Result |\n220\t|----------|-----------|--------|\n221\t| **Application restart** | Process exits, `_telegram_fallback` goes out of scope | ✅ **Automatic reset** - New process = fresh instance = default state |\n222\t| **Manual reset (future)** | Call `reset_first_failure_state()` method | ✅ **Intentional reset** - Set fields back to defaults without restart |\n223\t| **Recovery-based reset (future)** | After N consecutive successful sends | ✅ **Conditional reset** - Reset flag when bridge recovers |\n224\t\n225\t**No persistence across restarts is intentional:**\n226\t- First-failure detection is per-startup-diagnostic, not persistent state\n227\t- Each restart gets a fresh window to detect \"is the bridge down right now?\"\n228\t- If bridge stays down across restarts, each restart logs one WARNING (correct behavior)\n229\t\n230\t### Thread-Safety Implementation\n231\t\n232\tThe lock protects the check-and-set sequence:\n233\t\n234\t```python\n235\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n236\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Serialize access\n237\t        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n238\t            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n239\t            logger.warning(...)\n240\t            self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # ✅ Set under lock\n241\t            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n242\t            self._failure_count = 1\n243\t        else:\n244\t            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n245\t            logger.debug(...)\n246\t            self._failure_count += 1\n247\t        self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n248\t```\n249\t\n250\t**Why `asyncio.Lock`?**\n251\t- Designed for async/await contexts (FastAPI uses async)\n252\t- Non-blocking when lock is free\n253\t- Serializes the critical section (check + set)\n254\t- Minimal overhead: lock acquisition is fast\n255\t\n256\t### State Access Patterns\n257\t\n258\t**Read access (no lock needed for simple reads):**\n259\t```python\n260\tdef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n261\t    return {\n262\t        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n263\t        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,  # Safe to read\n264\t        # ...\n265\t    }\n266\t```\n267\t\n268\t**Write access (always under lock):**\n269\t```python\n270\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n271\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Required\n272\t        # ... modify state ...\n273\t```\n274\t\n275\t## Why This Design Works\n276\t\n277\t### 1. Matches Application Architecture\n278\t- FastAPI is async → `asyncio.Lock` is native\n279\t- Single-process deployment → singleton pattern is natural\n280\t- Module-level services → global accessor function\n281\t\n282\t### 2. Correct Lifecycle\n283\t- **Initialization**: On first use (lazy)\n284\t- **Lifetime**: Application lifetime (singleton)\n285\t- **Reset**: On application restart (automatic)\n286\t\n287\t### 3. Thread-Safe by Design\n288\t- Lock protects the critical section\n289\t- No race conditions possible\n290\t- Clear synchronization points\n291\t\n292\t### 4. Testable\n293\t```python\n294\t# Each test gets a fresh instance\n295\tasync def test_first_failure_logging():\n296\t    fallback = TelegramFallback()  # Fresh instance\n297\t    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error\")\n298\t    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n299\t```\n300\t\n301\t### 5. Simple and Maintainable\n302\t- All state lives in one place (the class that uses it)\n303\t- No external dependencies (databases, caches)\n304\t- Clear mental model (one instance, one state)\n305\t\n306\t## Implementation Checklist\n307\t\n308\tFor the implementation bead:\n309\t\n310\t- [ ] Add `asyncio.Lock` to `TelegramFallback.__init__()`\n311\t- [ ] Make `_handle_send_failure()` async (currently sync)\n312\t- [ ] Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n313\t- [ ] Ensure all state mutations are under lock protection\n314\t- [ ] Add tests for concurrent failures (use `asyncio.gather()`)\n315\t- [ ] Add test for first-failure flag reset after restart\n316\t\n317\t## Summary\n318\t\n319\t**Storage Location:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` class (singleton pattern)\n320\t\n321\t**Rationale:**\n322\t1. ✅ Thread-safe with `asyncio.Lock`\n323\t2. ✅ Clear lifecycle (init on first use, dies on process exit)\n324\t3. ✅ Natural reset on application restart\n325\t4. ✅ Accessible globally via `get_telegram_fallback()`\n326\t5. ✅ Encapsulated within the class that uses it\n327\t6. ✅ Testable (fresh instance per test)\n328\t7. ✅ Simple (no external dependencies)\n329\t\n330\t**Rejected Options:**\n331\t- Module-level variable (not thread-safe)\n332\t- Class attribute (unnecessary complexity)\n333\t- Request context (wrong lifecycle)\n334\t- Database (wrong lifecycle, overkill)\n335\t\n336\t**Reset Behavior:**\n337\t- Automatic reset on application restart (process exit → new instance)\n338\t- Future: Manual reset via method call\n339\t- Future: Recovery-based reset after N consecutive successes\n340\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"56d9ffc9-24ed-4173-9257-9d7110998b7f","uuid":"0927bffe-d812-4e3b-b01b-ac0f976838a5","timestamp":"2026-07-19T22:43:38.723Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/notes/adc-2duz-state-storage-design.md","content":"# First-Failure State Storage Design\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document evaluates storage options for the first-failure tracking state in the async FastAPI application and documents the recommended approach with rationale.\n\n## Design Requirements\n\n### Functional Requirements\n1. State must track first-failure occurrence across application lifetime\n2. State must survive from application startup until shutdown\n3. Only ONE first-failure event should be detected per startup sequence\n4. State must be accessible to all `send_message()` calls\n\n### Non-Functional Requirements\n1. **Thread-safety**: Multiple concurrent async requests must not corrupt state\n2. **Performance**: Minimal overhead on the hot path (Telegram sends)\n3. **Lifecycle clarity**: Clear initialization and reset behavior\n4. **Simplicity**: Easy to understand, test, and maintain\n\n## Storage Options Evaluation\n\n### Option 1: Module-Level Variable\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\n# In src/telegram/fallback.py\n_first_failure_state = {\n    \"has_logged_first_failure\": False,\n    \"failure_count\": 0,\n    \"first_failure_timestamp\": None,\n    \"last_failure_timestamp\": None,\n}\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- Simple to understand\n- Accessible from anywhere in the module\n- No instance overhead\n\n**Cons:**\n- ❌ **Not thread-safe**: Concurrent async coroutines can race on check-and-set\n- ❌ **Hard to test**: Global state pollutes tests; requires manual reset\n- ❌ **No encapsulation**: Any code can mutate state directly\n- ❌ **No lock integration**: Would need a separate global lock object\n\n**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Not thread-safe in async context\n\n---\n\n### Option 2: Class Attribute (Shared State)\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    # Class attribute - shared across ALL instances\n    _has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n    _first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = None\n    \n    @classmethod\n    async def get_class_lock(cls):\n        if cls._first_failure_lock is None:\n            cls._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n        return cls._first_failure_lock\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- State shared across all instances (if multiple were created)\n- Class-level organization\n\n**Cons:**\n- ❌ **Unnecessary complexity**: `TelegramFallback` is a singleton anyway\n- ❌ **Lock initialization timing**: Class attrs init at import time, before event loop\n- ❌ **Testing complexity**: Hard to reset between tests\n- ❌ **Surprising behavior**: Shared state across instances is non-obvious\n\n**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Adds complexity without benefit\n\n---\n\n### Option 3: Instance Variable (Per-Instance State)\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        # Instance variables\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._failure_count = 0\n        self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n        self._last_failure_timestamp = None\n        self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- ✅ **Thread-safe with lock**: `asyncio.Lock` serializes concurrent access\n- ✅ **Clear lifecycle**: Initializes when instance creates, dies when instance destroyed\n- ✅ **Singleton pattern**: `get_telegram_fallback()` ensures one instance per app\n- ✅ **Encapsulated**: State lives inside the class that uses it\n- ✅ **Testable**: Each test can create a fresh instance\n- ✅ **Natural reset**: Application restart = new instance = reset state\n\n**Cons:**\n- None significant for this use case\n\n**Verdict:** ✅ **RECOMMENDED** - Matches all requirements\n\n---\n\n### Option 4: Request Context (Per-Request State)\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\nfrom starlette.requests import Request\n\nasync def send_message(request: Request, chat_id, message):\n    # Store in request.state\n    if not hasattr(request.state, \"first_failure_logged\"):\n        request.state.first_failure_logged = True\n        logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- Natural isolation per request\n\n**Cons:**\n- ❌ **Wrong lifecycle**: Request-scoped means state resets EVERY request\n- ❌ **Violates requirement**: Need ONE first-failure per startup, not per request\n- ❌ **Impossible**: Would need to persist across requests (defeats the purpose)\n\n**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Wrong lifecycle for this requirement\n\n---\n\n### Option 5: Database Persistence\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\n# Store in SQLite or PostgreSQL\nCREATE TABLE first_failure_state (\n    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,\n    has_logged_first_failure BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,\n    failure_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,\n    first_failure_timestamp TIMESTAMP,\n    last_failure_timestamp TIMESTAMP\n);\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- Survives application restarts\n- Queryable for monitoring\n\n**Cons:**\n- ❌ **Wrong lifecycle**: First-failure should RESET on restart, not persist\n- ❌ **Performance overhead**: Database write on every failure (hot path)\n- ❌ **Overkill**: No need for persistence; state is transient by design\n- ❌ **Complexity**: Adds database dependency for simple boolean flag\n\n**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Adds complexity for wrong lifecycle\n\n---\n\n## Recommended Design: Instance Variable with Singleton\n\n### Storage Location\n\n**Primary storage:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` class\n\n```python\n# src/telegram/fallback.py\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        # ... existing initialization ...\n        \n        # First-failure tracking state (instance variables)\n        self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n        self._failure_count: int = 0\n        self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n        self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n        \n        # Thread-safety: async lock for concurrent access\n        self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n```\n\n### Global Access via Singleton\n\nThe application uses a module-level singleton pattern:\n\n```python\n# Global instance (module-level)\n_telegram_fallback: Optional[TelegramFallback] = None\n\ndef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n    \"\"\"Get or create the global Telegram fallback instance.\"\"\"\n    global _telegram_fallback\n    if _telegram_fallback is None:\n        _telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    return _telegram_fallback\n```\n\n**Why this works:**\n1. **First call**: Creates the singleton instance, initializes state\n2. **Subsequent calls**: Returns the same instance with preserved state\n3. **Application lifetime**: One instance lives for entire app runtime\n4. **Application restart**: Process exit → `_telegram_fallback = None` → fresh state on next startup\n\n### Initialization Timing\n\n| Event | What Happens | State Value |\n|-------|-------------|-------------|\n| **Application startup** | `get_telegram_fallback()` called for first time | All fields at defaults |\n| **Module import** | `TelegramFallback` class defined | No instance yet |\n| **First use** | `send_message()` → `get_telegram_fallback()` → `__init__()` | Instance created, state initialized |\n| **First failure** | `_handle_send_failure()` under lock | `_has_logged_first_failure = True` |\n| **Application restart** | Process exits, new process starts | Fresh instance = fresh state |\n\n### Reset Behavior\n\n| Scenario | Mechanism | Result |\n|----------|-----------|--------|\n| **Application restart** | Process exits, `_telegram_fallback` goes out of scope | ✅ **Automatic reset** - New process = fresh instance = default state |\n| **Manual reset (future)** | Call `reset_first_failure_state()` method | ✅ **Intentional reset** - Set fields back to defaults without restart |\n| **Recovery-based reset (future)** | After N consecutive successful sends | ✅ **Conditional reset** - Reset flag when bridge recovers |\n\n**No persistence across restarts is intentional:**\n- First-failure detection is per-startup-diagnostic, not persistent state\n- Each restart gets a fresh window to detect \"is the bridge down right now?\"\n- If bridge stays down across restarts, each restart logs one WARNING (correct behavior)\n\n### Thread-Safety Implementation\n\nThe lock protects the check-and-set sequence:\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Serialize access\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n            logger.warning(...)\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # ✅ Set under lock\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n            self._failure_count = 1\n        else:\n            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n            logger.debug(...)\n            self._failure_count += 1\n        self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n```\n\n**Why `asyncio.Lock`?**\n- Designed for async/await contexts (FastAPI uses async)\n- Non-blocking when lock is free\n- Serializes the critical section (check + set)\n- Minimal overhead: lock acquisition is fast\n\n### State Access Patterns\n\n**Read access (no lock needed for simple reads):**\n```python\ndef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n    return {\n        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,  # Safe to read\n        # ...\n    }\n```\n\n**Write access (always under lock):**\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Required\n        # ... modify state ...\n```\n\n## Why This Design Works\n\n### 1. Matches Application Architecture\n- FastAPI is async → `asyncio.Lock` is native\n- Single-process deployment → singleton pattern is natural\n- Module-level services → global accessor function\n\n### 2. Correct Lifecycle\n- **Initialization**: On first use (lazy)\n- **Lifetime**: Application lifetime (singleton)\n- **Reset**: On application restart (automatic)\n\n### 3. Thread-Safe by Design\n- Lock protects the critical section\n- No race conditions possible\n- Clear synchronization points\n\n### 4. Testable\n```python\n# Each test gets a fresh instance\nasync def test_first_failure_logging():\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()  # Fresh instance\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error\")\n    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n```\n\n### 5. Simple and Maintainable\n- All state lives in one place (the class that uses it)\n- No external dependencies (databases, caches)\n- Clear mental model (one instance, one state)\n\n## Implementation Checklist\n\nFor the implementation bead:\n\n- [ ] Add `asyncio.Lock` to `TelegramFallback.__init__()`\n- [ ] Make `_handle_send_failure()` async (currently sync)\n- [ ] Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n- [ ] Ensure all state mutations are under lock protection\n- [ ] Add tests for concurrent failures (use `asyncio.gather()`)\n- [ ] Add test for first-failure flag reset after restart\n\n## Summary\n\n**Storage Location:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` class (singleton pattern)\n\n**Rationale:**\n1. ✅ Thread-safe with `asyncio.Lock`\n2. ✅ Clear lifecycle (init on first use, dies on process exit)\n3. ✅ Natural reset on application restart\n4. ✅ Accessible globally via `get_telegram_fallback()`\n5. ✅ Encapsulated within the class that uses it\n6. ✅ Testable (fresh instance per test)\n7. ✅ Simple (no external dependencies)\n\n**Rejected Options:**\n- Module-level variable (not thread-safe)\n- Class attribute (unnecessary complexity)\n- Request context (wrong lifecycle)\n- Database (wrong lifecycle, overkill)\n\n**Reset Behavior:**\n- Automatic reset on application restart (process exit → new instance)\n- Future: Manual reset via method call\n- Future: Recovery-based reset after N consecutive successes\n","numLines":340,"startLine":1,"totalLines":340}}}

def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        import os\n        self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\n            \"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", self.DEFAULT_BRIDGE_URL\n        )\n        # Track bridge reachability state\n        self._is_reachable = None  # None = unknown, True = reachable, False = unreachable\n        self._last_failure_logged = None  # Track when we last send_message(\n        self,\n        chat_id: int | str,\n        message: str,\n        parse_mode: str = \"HTML\",\n    ) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Send a message to a Telegram chat.\n\n        Args:\n            chat_id: Telegram chat ID (int or str)\n            message: Message text to send\n            parse_mode: Parse mode for formatting (Markdown, HTML, etc.)\n\n        Returns:\n            True if successful, False otherwise.\n        \"\"\"\n        try:\n            async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:\n                # telegram-claude-bridge proxy API uses /send endpoint\n                # Contract: {chat_id, text, parse_mode?, thread_id?, reply_to_message_id?}\n                response = await client.post(\n                    f\"{self.bridge_url}/send\",\n                    json={\n                        \"chat_id\": int(chat_id) if isinstance(chat_id, str) else chat_id,\n                        \"text\": message,\n                        \"parse_mode\": parse_mode,\n                    },\n                    timeout=10.0,\n                )\n\n                if response.status_code == 200:\n                    logger.info(f\"Sent Telegram message to chat {chat_id}\")\n                    self._is_reachable = True  # Update reachability state\n                    return True\n                else:\n                    error_msg = f\"status {response.status_code} - {response.text}\"\n                    self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n                    return False\n\n        except httpx.RequestError as e:\n            error_msg = f\"request error: {e}\"\n            self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n            return False\n        except Exception as e:\n            error_msg = f\"unexpected error: {e}\"\n            self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n            return False\n\n    async def send_result(self, chat_id: int | str, result: dict) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Send a structured result to Telegram.\n\n        Args:\n            chat_id: Telegram chat ID\n            result: Result dict with keys: summary, urgency, data\n\n        Formats the result as a readable message and sends mapping which is not\n        currently implemented. telegram-claude-bridge uses pull-based architecture\n        (manages sessions internally per forum topic), not push-based message delivery.\n Logs a WARNING only for the very first failure after startup.\n        All subsequent failures are logged at DEBUG level to avoid spam.\n\n        Args:\n            error_context: Details about the error (status code, error message, etc.)\n        \"\"\"\n        self._is_reachable = False\n        self._failure_count += 1\n        now = datetime.now()\n\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # First failure after startup - log at WARNING level\n            logger.warning(\n                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n            )\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n            self._last_failure_logged = now\n        else:\n            # Subsequent failures - log at DEBUG level to avoid spam\n            logger.debug(\n                f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count} at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}.\"\n            )\n\n    def _format_result_message(self, result: dict) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Format a result as a Telegram message.\"\"\"\n        summary = result.get(\"summary\", \"Result available\")\n title = exception.get(\"title\", \"Attention Required\")\n        urgency = exception.get(\"urgency\", \"normal\")\n        context = exception.get(\"context\", \"\")\n        options =        \"\"\"Format a workload summary as a Telegram message.\"\"\"\n        pending = summary.get(\"pending_intents\", 0)\n        new_results = summary.get(\"new_results\", 0)\n        exceptions = So my synthesis must use:\n- The `asyncio.Lock` + await-free `_record_failure_locked` plain-def helper pattern (from adc-50ld)\n- The detection logic with `was_first` returned from the locked helper (from adc-12bt)\n- The four field structure (from adc-65l3)\n- Instance-variable singleton storage (from adc-2duz)\n\nThere's a key tension to resolve in the synthesis: adc-65l3 and adc-2duz (the older, 2026-07-08 designs) show `_handle_send_failure` doing the logging **inside** the lock with `async with self._first_failure_lock:` wrapping the whole thing including the `logger.warning`. But adc-50ld and adc-12bt (the newer, 2026-07-19 authoritative designs) refactor this into:\n1. `async _handle_send_failure` acquires lock, calls `_record_failure_locked` (plain def, returns was_first bool)\n2. Notification/logging I/O happens AFTER lock release\n\nWait — there's a subtle distinction. adc-50ld's pseudo-code shows the WARNING log **inside** `_record_failure_locked` (the plain def helper). Let me re-check.\n\nFrom adc-50ld:\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n    was_first = False\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n        was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context)  # plain def; no await\n    if was_first:\n        await self._notify_first_failure(error_context)         # I/O outside the lock\n```\n\nAnd in adc-50ld §3, the existing `_handle_send_failure` body (which logs warning synchronously) is shown and noted that \"stdlib logging — blocking I/O, but does NOT yield\". So stdlib logging inside the locked section is acceptable (it doesn't yield).\n\nBut adc-12bt §5.2 shows `_record_failure_locked` including the `logger.warning(...)` call inside it:\n```python\ndef _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\n    now = datetime.now()\n    self._is_reachable = False\n    self._failure_count += 1\n    self._last_failure_timestamp = now\n    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n        self._first_failure_timestamp = now\n        logger.warning(...)   # <-- inside the locked helper\n        return True\n    return False\n```\n\nSo the WARNING log IS inside `_record_failure_locked` (synchronous, fine). And the separate `_notify_first_failure` is the **side-channel** notification (e.g., to a different transport) that runs after lock release. So there are two things:\n1. The WARNING log (synchronous stdlib) — inside locked helper, fine\n2. The `_notify_first_failure` side-channel I/O — after lock release\n\nThis is an important distinction for my synthesis to get right.\n\nNow there's also a field-naming reconciliation:\n- Current code: `_last_failure_logged` (only updated on first failure)\n- adc-65l3 standardizes: `_last_failure_timestamp` (updated on every failure)\n- adc-65l3 adds: `_first_failure_timestamp` (set-once)\n\nAnd the `_failure_count` update:\n- Current code: `self._failure_count += 1` happens BEFORE the if/else (so on first failure, count goes to 1)\n- adc-65l3/adc-2duz older sketches set `_failure_count = 1` in the first-failure branch and `_failure_count += 1` in the else branch — which is redundant/buggy if the increment already happened before. \n- adc-12bt/adc-50ld authoritative: `self._failure_count += 1` first (unconditional, under lock), THEN check the flag. This is cleaner. I'll use this.\n\nLet me verify the existing parent-bead design doc to see if there's an earlier synthesis I should supersede, and check the docs/ versions. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_5796043b9a604374a39f4b76","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# First-Failure Tracking Mechanism Design\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\tThis document defines the design for tracking and detecting the **FIRST** Telegram send failure after application startup in the aide-de-camp (adc) FastAPI application.\n5\t\n6\t## Current State\n7\t\n8\tThe existing implementation in `src/telegram/fallback.py` already includes a basic first-failure tracking mechanism:\n9\t\n10\t```python\n11\t# Current implementation\n12\tself._has_logged_first_failure = False  # Track if we've logged the first failure after startup\n13\tself._failure_count = 0\n14\tself._last_failure_logged = None\n15\t\n16\tdef _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n17\t    self._is_reachable = False\n18\t    self._failure_count += 1\n19\t    now = datetime.now()\n20\t\n21\t    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n22\t        # First failure after startup - log at WARNING level\n23\t        logger.warning(...)\n24\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n25\t        self._last_failure_logged = now\n26\t    else:\n27\t        # Subsequent failures - log at DEBUG level\n28\t        logger.debug(...)\n29\t```\n30\t\n31\t## Design Requirements\n32\t\n33\t### Functional Requirements\n34\t1. **First-failure detection**: Log a WARNING on the very first Telegram send failure after application startup\n35\t2. **Subsequent failure suppression**: All subsequent failures should be logged at DEBUG level only\n36\t3. **No false positives**: Only one WARNING should ever be logged per startup sequence\n37\t4. **State persistence**: State should persist across the application lifetime\n38\t\n39\t### Non-Functional Requirements\n40\t1. **Thread-safety**: The application is async FastAPI; multiple concurrent requests could trigger Telegram sends simultaneously\n41\t2. **Race-condition prevention**: Multiple concurrent failures should not result in multiple WARNING logs\n42\t3. **Performance**: Minimal overhead on the hot path (Telegram send operations)\n43\t4. **Recovery awareness**: Option to reset first-failure state after successful sends (future enhancement)\n44\t\n45\t## Proposed Design\n46\t\n47\t### Architecture\n48\t\n49\t```\n50\t┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n51\t│                     TelegramFallback Instance                │\n52\t├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n53\t│                                                               │\n54\t│  FirstFailureTracker (inner class or module)                 │\n55\t│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │\n56\t│  │ State:                                               │    │\n57\t│  │   - _has_logged_first_failure: bool                 │    │\n58\t│  │   - _failure_count: int                             │    │\n59\t│  │   - _first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock (thread-safe) │    │\n60\t│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │\n61\t│                                                               │\n62\t└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n63\t```\n64\t\n65\t### State Storage\n66\t\n67\t**Location**: Per-instance state in `TelegramFallback.__init__()`\n68\t\n69\t**Rationale**:\n70\t- `TelegramFallback` is a module-level singleton (`get_telegram_fallback()`)\n71\t- Only one instance exists per application lifetime\n72\t- State survives for the entire application runtime\n73\t- State resets naturally on application restart\n74\t\n75\t**State Variables**:\n76\t```python\n77\tself._failure_tracker = FirstFailureTracker()  # Encapsulated tracker\n78\t```\n79\t\n80\t### Thread-Safety Design\n81\t\n82\t**Problem**: In async FastAPI, multiple `send_message()` calls can execute concurrently. Without synchronization:\n83\t- Two concurrent failures could both check `if not self._has_logged_first_failure` before either sets it\n84\t- This causes race conditions → multiple WARNING logs for the \"first\" failure\n85\t\n86\t**Solution**: Use `asyncio.Lock` to serialize the first-failure check-and-set operation:\n87\t\n88\t```python\n89\tclass FirstFailureTracker:\n90\t    def __init__(self):\n91\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n92\t        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()  # Async lock for concurrent access\n93\t\n94\t    async def record_failure(self, error_context: str) -> str:\n95\t        \"\"\"\n96\t        Record a failure and return the appropriate log level.\n97\t        \n98\t        Returns:\n99\t            'WARNING' if this is the first failure, 'DEBUG' otherwise\n100\t        \"\"\"\n101\t        async with self._lock:\n102\t            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n103\t                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n104\t                return 'WARNING'\n105\t            return 'DEBUG'\n106\t```\n107\t\n108\t**Why asyncio.Lock?**\n109\t- Designed for async/await contexts\n110\t- Non-blocking when the lock is free\n111\t- Prevents race conditions on the first-failure flag\n112\t- Minimal overhead: lock acquisition is fast, only contends briefly at startup\n113\t\n114\t### Initialization\n115\t\n116\tThe tracker initializes on first access via `get_telegram_fallback()`:\n117\t\n118\t```python\n119\t# In TelegramFallback.__init__()\n120\tself._failure_tracker = FirstFailureTracker()\n121\t```\n122\t\n123\tState is **NOT** persisted across restarts — this is intentional. Each application restart gets a fresh first-failure tracking window.\n124\t\n125\t### Usage Pattern\n126\t\n127\t```python\n128\tasync def send_message(self, chat_id, message, parse_mode=\"HTML\"):\n129\t    try:\n130\t        # ... send logic ...\n131\t        if response.status_code == 200:\n132\t            return True\n133\t        else:\n134\t            error_msg = f\"status {response.status_code}\"\n135\t            await self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n136\t            return False\n137\t    except Exception as e:\n138\t        await self._handle_send_failure(str(e))\n139\t        return False\n140\t\n141\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str):\n142\t    log_level = await self._failure_tracker.record_failure(error_context)\n143\t    \n144\t    if log_level == 'WARNING':\n145\t        logger.warning(\n146\t            f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n147\t            f\"Error: {error_context}. \"\n148\t            f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n149\t        )\n150\t    else:\n151\t        logger.debug(\n152\t            f\"Repeated Telegram send failure at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n153\t            f\"Error: {error_context}\"\n154\t        )\n155\t```\n156\t\n157\t### Race Condition Scenarios\n158\t\n159\t**Scenario 1: Two concurrent failures at startup**\n160\t```\n161\tTime  T1: Request A fails, acquires lock\n162\tTime  T2: Request B fails, waits for lock\n163\tTime  T3: Request A sets flag, releases lock, logs WARNING\n164\tTime  T4: Request B acquires lock, sees flag=True, logs DEBUG\n165\t```\n166\t✅ **Correct**: Only one WARNING logged\n167\t\n168\t**Scenario 2: Failure followed by success, then failure again**\n169\t```\n170\tTime  T1: Request A fails, logs WARNING (flag=True)\n171\tTime  T2: Request B succeeds (flag remains True)\n172\tTime  T3: Request C fails, logs DEBUG (flag already True)\n173\t```\n174\t✅ **Correct**: Only first failure ever gets WARNING\n175\t\n176\t**Scenario 3: Multiple concurrent failures after first failure**\n177\t```\n178\tTime  T1: First failure already happened (flag=True)\n179\tTime  T2: Requests A, B, C all fail concurrently\n180\tTime  T3: All acquire lock, see flag=True, all log DEBUG\n181\t```\n182\t✅ **Correct**: No WARNINGs, only DEBUG logs\n183\t\n184\t## Implementation Guidance\n185\t\n186\t### For the next bead (implementation):\n187\t\n188\t1. **Create a new module** `src/telegram/first_failure_tracker.py` with the `FirstFailureTracker` class\n189\t\n190\t2. **Refactor `TelegramFallback`**:\n191\t   - Replace `_has_logged_first_failure` with `_failure_tracker: FirstFailureTracker`\n192\t   - Make `_handle_send_failure()` async (currently synchronous)\n193\t   - Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n194\t\n195\t3. **Add tests** in `tests/test_first_failure_tracker.py`:\n196\t   - Test concurrent first failures (use `asyncio.gather()`)\n197\t   - Test subsequent failures are DEBUG\n198\t   - Test flag resets after restart (manual verification)\n199\t\n200\t### Migration from current implementation\n201\t\n202\t**Current code**:\n203\t```python\n204\t# Instance variables in TelegramFallback.__init__\n205\tself._has_logged_first_failure = False\n206\tself._last_failure_logged = None\n207\tself._failure_count = 0\n208\t\n209\t# Synchronous handler\n210\tdef _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n211\t    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n212\t        logger.warning(...)\n213\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n214\t    else:\n215\t        logger.debug(...)\n216\t```\n217\t\n218\t**New code**:\n219\t```python\n220\t# Encapsulated tracker\n221\tself._failure_tracker = FirstFailureTracker()\n222\t\n223\t# Async handler with lock protection\n224\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n225\t    log_level = await self._failure_tracker.record_failure(error_context)\n226\t    if log_level == 'WARNING':\n227\t        logger.warning(...)\n228\t    else:\n229\t        logger.debug(...)\n230\t```\n231\t\n232\t## Future Enhancements\n233\t\n234\t### Recovery-based reset (optional future feature)\n235\tTrack whether the Telegram bridge has recovered, and reset the first-failure flag after N successful sends:\n236\t\n237\t```python\n238\tclass FirstFailureTracker:\n239\t    def __init__(self, reset_after_success_count: int = 5):\n240\t        self._reset_threshold = reset_after_success_count\n241\t        self._success_count_since_failure = 0\n242\t\n243\t    async def record_success(self):\n244\t        async with self._lock:\n245\t            self._success_count_since_failure += 1\n246\t            if self._success_count_since_failure >= self._reset_threshold:\n247\t                self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n248\t```\n249\t\n250\tThis would allow WARNING-level logging for \"new\" failure episodes after recovery, not just the very first failure at application startup.\n251\t\n252\t## Summary\n253\t\n254\tThe proposed design:\n255\t1. ✅ Encapsulates first-failure tracking in a dedicated class\n256\t2. ✅ Uses `asyncio.Lock` for thread-safety in async FastAPI context\n257\t3. ✅ Stores state per-instance (singleton pattern ensures global visibility)\n258\t4. ✅ Initializes on first access, resets on application restart\n259\t5. ✅ Prevents race conditions on concurrent failures\n260\t6. ✅ Maintains minimal performance overhead\n261\t7. ✅ Provides clear migration path from existing First-Failure Tracking Mechanism Design\n\n## Overview\nThis document defines the design for tracking and detecting the **FIRST** Telegram send failure after application startup in the aide-de-camp (adc) FastAPI application.\n\n## Current State\n\nThe existing implementation in `src/telegram/fallback.py` already includes a basic first-failure tracking mechanism:\n\n```python\n# Current implementation\nself._has_logged_first_failure = False  # Track if we've logged the first failure after startup\nself._failure_count = 0\nself._last_failure_logged = None\n\ndef _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    self._is_reachable = False\n    self._failure_count += 1\n    now = datetime.now()\n\n    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n        # First failure after startup - log at WARNING level\n        logger.warning(...)\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n        self._last_failure_logged = now\n    else:\n        # Subsequent failures - log at DEBUG level\n        logger.debug(...)\n```\n\n## Design Requirements\n\n### Functional Requirements\n1. **First-failure detection**: Log a WARNING on the very first Telegram send failure after application startup\n2. **Subsequent failure suppression**: All subsequent failures should be logged at DEBUG level only\n3. **No false positives**: Only one WARNING should ever be logged per startup sequence\n4. **State persistence**: State should persist across the application lifetime\n\n### Non-Functional Requirements\n1. **Thread-safety**: The application is async FastAPI; multiple concurrent requests could trigger Telegram sends simultaneously\n2. **Race-condition prevention**: Multiple concurrent failures should not result in multiple WARNING logs\n3. **Performance**: Minimal overhead on the hot path (Telegram send operations)\n4. **Recovery awareness**: Option to reset first-failure state after successful sends (future enhancement)\n\n## Proposed Design\n\n### Architecture\n\n```\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│                     TelegramFallback Instance                │\n├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n│                                                               │\n│  FirstFailureTracker (inner class or module)                 │\n│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │\n│  │ State:                                               │    │\n│  │   - _has_logged_first_failure: bool                 │    │\n│  │   - _failure_count: int                             │    │\n│  │   - _first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock (thread-safe) │    │\n│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │\n│                                                               │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n### State Storage\n\n**Location**: Per-instance state in `TelegramFallback.__init__()`\n\n**Rationale**:\n- `TelegramFallback` is a module-level singleton (`get_telegram_fallback()`)\n- Only one instance exists per application lifetime\n- State survives for the entire application runtime\n- State resets naturally on application restart\n\n**State Variables**:\n```python\nself._failure_tracker = FirstFailureTracker()  # Encapsulated tracker\n```\n\n### Thread-Safety Design\n\n**Problem**: In async FastAPI, multiple `send_message()` calls can execute concurrently. Without synchronization:\n- Two concurrent failures could both check `if not self._has_logged_first_failure` before either sets it\n- This causes race conditions → multiple WARNING logs for the \"first\" failure\n\n**Solution**: Use `asyncio.Lock` to serialize the first-failure check-and-set operation:\n\n```python\nclass FirstFailureTracker:\n    def __init__(self):\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()  # Async lock for concurrent access\n\n    async def record_failure(self, error_context: str) -> str:\n        \"\"\"\n        Record a failure and return the appropriate log level.\n        \n        Returns:\n            'WARNING' if this is the first failure, 'DEBUG' otherwise\n        \"\"\"\n        async with self._lock:\n            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n                return 'WARNING'\n            return 'DEBUG'\n```\n\n**Why asyncio.Lock?**\n- Designed for async/await contexts\n- Non-blocking when the lock is free\n- Prevents race conditions on the first-failure flag\n- Minimal overhead: lock acquisition is fast, only contends briefly at startup\n\n### Initialization\n\nThe tracker initializes on first access via `get_telegram_fallback()`:\n\n```python\n# In TelegramFallback.__init__()\nself._failure_tracker = FirstFailureTracker()\n```\n\nState is **NOT** persisted across restarts — this is intentional. Each application restart gets a fresh first-failure tracking window.\n\n### Usage Pattern\n\n```python\nasync def send_message(self, chat_id, message, parse_mode=\"HTML\"):\n    try:\n        # ... send logic ...\n        if response.status_code == 200:\n            return True\n        else:\n            error_msg = f\"status {response.status_code}\"\n            await self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n            return False\n    except Exception as e:\n        await self._handle_send_failure(str(e))\n        return False\n\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str):\n    log_level = await self._failure_tracker.record_failure(error_context)\n    \n    if log_level == 'WARNING':\n        logger.warning(\n            f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n            f\"Error: {error_context}. \"\n            f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n        )\n    else:\n        logger.debug(\n            f\"Repeated Telegram send failure at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n            f\"Error: {error_context}\"\n        )\n```\n\n### Race Condition Scenarios\n\n**Scenario 1: Two concurrent failures at startup**\n```\nTime  T1: Request A fails, acquires lock\nTime  T2: Request B fails, waits for lock\nTime  T3: Request A sets flag, releases lock, logs WARNING\nTime  T4: Request B acquires lock, sees flag=True, logs DEBUG\n```\n✅ **Correct**: Only one WARNING logged\n\n**Scenario 2: Failure followed by success, then failure again**\n```\nTime  T1: Request A fails, logs WARNING (flag=True)\nTime  T2: Request B succeeds (flag remains True)\nTime  T3: Request C fails, logs DEBUG (flag already True)\n```\n✅ **Correct**: Only first failure ever gets WARNING\n\n**Scenario 3: Multiple concurrent failures after first failure**\n```\nTime  T1: First failure already happened (flag=True)\nTime  T2: Requests A, B, C all fail concurrently\nTime  T3: All acquire lock, see flag=True, all log DEBUG\n```\n✅ **Correct**: No WARNINGs, only DEBUG logs\n\n## Implementation Guidance\n\n### For the next bead (implementation):\n\n1. **Create a new module** `src/telegram/first_failure_tracker.py` with the `FirstFailureTracker` class\n\n2. **Refactor `TelegramFallback`**:\n   - Replace `_has_logged_first_failure` with `_failure_tracker: FirstFailureTracker`\n   - Make `_handle_send_failure()` async (currently synchronous)\n   - Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n\n3. **Add tests** in `tests/test_first_failure_tracker.py`:\n   - Test concurrent first failures (use `asyncio.gather()`)\n   - Test subsequent failures are DEBUG\n   - Test flag resets after restart (manual verification)\n\n### Migration from current implementation\n\n**Current code**:\n```python\n# Instance variables in TelegramFallback.__init__\nself._has_logged_first_failure = False\nself._last_failure_logged = None\nself._failure_count = 0\n\n# Synchronous handler\ndef _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n        logger.warning(...)\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n    else:\n        logger.debug(...)\n```\n\n**New code**:\n```python\n# Encapsulated tracker\nself._failure_tracker = FirstFailureTracker()\n\n# Async handler with lock protection\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    log_level = await self._failure_tracker.record_failure(error_context)\n    if log_level == 'WARNING':\n        logger.warning(...)\n    else:\n        logger.debug(...)\n```\n\n## Future Enhancements\n\n### Recovery-based reset (optional future feature)\nTrack whether the Telegram bridge has recovered, and reset the first-failure flag after N successful sends:\n\n```python\nclass FirstFailureTracker:\n    def __init__(self, reset_after_success_count: int = 5):\n        self._reset_threshold = reset_after_success_count\n        self._success_count_since_failure = 0\n\n    async def record_success(self):\n        async with self._lock:\n            self._success_count_since_failure += 1\n            if self._success_count_since_failure >= self._reset_threshold:\n                self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n```\n\nThis would allow WARNING-level logging for \"new\" failure episodes after recovery, not just the very first failure at application startup.\n\n## Summary\n\nThe proposed design:\n1. ✅ Encapsulates first-failure tracking in a dedicated class\n2. ✅ Uses `asyncio.Lock` for thread-safety in async FastAPI context\n3. ✅ Stores state per-instance (singleton pattern ensures global visibility)\n4. ✅ Initializes on first access, resets on application restart\n5. ✅ Prevents race conditions on concurrent failures\n6. ✅ Maintains minimal performance overhead\n7. ✅ Provides clear migration path from existing implementation\n","numLines":262,"startLine":1,"totalLines":262}}}

The state prevents duplicate notifications while maintaining diagnostic information.\n\n## State Structure\n\n```python\n@dataclass\nclass FirstFailureState:\n    \"\"\"Tracks the first Telegram send failure after startup.\n    \n    This state is used to detect and report only the FIRST failure,\n    preventing notification spam on subsequent send failures.\n    \"\"\"\n    # I'll be thorough but disciplined — reference child docs for full depth rather than copy-pasting them.\n\nFor the code, I need to produce the canonical integrated version. Let me carefully construct it from adc-12bt §5.2 (the most refined pseudo-code) plus adc-65l3 field names plus adc-2duz storage + adc-50ld structural rules:\n\n```python\nimport asyncio\nfrom datetime import datetime\nfrom typing import Optional\n\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        import os\n        self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\n            \"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", self.DEFAULT_BRIDGE_URL\n        )\n        # --- Reachability (separate logical object; NOT under the first-failure lock) ---\n        self._is_reachable = None  # None=unknown, True=reachable, False=unreachable\n\n        # --- First-failure record (adc-65l3 fields, adc-2duz storage) ---\n        self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n        self._failure_count: int = 0\n        self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None  # set-once (NEW — adc-65l3)\n        self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None   # renamed from _last_failure_logged; updated every failure\n\n        # --- Thread-safety (adc-50ld): one instance lock, await-free critical section ---\n        self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n\n    async def send_message(self, chat_id, message, parse_mode=\"HTML\") -> bool:\n        try:\n            async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:\n                response = await client.post(...)\n                if response.status_code == 200:\n                    self._is_reachable = True\n                    return True\n                else:\n                    error_msg = f\"status {response.status_code} - {response.text}\"\n                    await self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)   # now awaited\n                    return False\n        except httpx.RequestError as e:\n            await self._handle_send_failure(f\"request error: {e}\")\n            return False\n        except Exception as e:\n            await self._handle_send_failure(f\"unexpected error: {e}\")\n            return False\n\n    async def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n        \"\"\"Reactive detection entry point. Called only from send_message failure branches (adc-12bt).\"\"\"\n        was_first = False\n        async with self._first_failure_lock:                          # adc-50ld\n            was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context)    # plain def; no await (adc-50ld §5.5)\n        # ---- lock released; detection decision is final below ----\n        if was_first:\n            await self._notify_first_failure(error_context)           # side-channel I/O (adc-12bt §7.4)\n        # (the per-failure DEBUG line is emitted inside _record_failure_locked for losers)\n\n    def _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"Caller MUST hold _first_failure_lock. Sync on purpose — no await (adc-50ld §5.5).\n\n        Returns True iff THIS call performed the _has_logged_first_failure\n        False→True flip (i.e. this is the first failure of the startup). (adc-12bt §2)\n        \"\"\"\n        now = datetime.now()\n        self._is_reachable = False                       # out of lock's *logical* scope but lives here for locality\n        self._failure_count += 1                         # unconditional (adc-12bt §5.2)\n        self._last_failure_timestamp = now               # every failure (adc-65l3 rename)\n\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:           # the predicate (adc-12bt §2)\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True        # the claim — atomic w/ the read, under lock\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = now          # set-once (adc-65l3)\n            logger.warning(\n                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context or 'unknown error'}. \"\n                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n            )\n            return True                                  # winner\n        logger.debug(\n            f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count} \"\n            f\"at {self.bridge_url}. Error: {error_context or 'unknown error'}.\"\n        )\n        return False                                     # loser → suppressed\n\n    async def _notify_first_failure(self, error_context: str) -> None:\n        \"\"\"Deliver the once-per-startup alert over a SIDE CHANNEL (adc-12bt §7.4).\n\n        MUST NOT call self.send_message(...) — notifying over the bridge that just\n        failed is self-defeating and would pollute the failure record. Exact channel\n        is the notification-implementation bead's concern; this method is the seam.\n        \"\"\"\n        # e.g. structured log sink / separate transport. Out of scope for v1 body;\n        # v1 may be a no-op or stderr write until the channel is chosen.\n        ...\n\n    def get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n        \"\"\"Lock-free read (adc-50ld §5.5 rule 6; Race 5 tolerated as stale-but-consistent).\"\"\"\n        return {\n            \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n            \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n            \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n            \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,\n            \"first_failure_timestamp\": self._first_failure_timestamp.isoformat()\n                if self._first_failure_timestamp else None,\n            \"last_failure_timestamp\": self._last_failure_timestamp.isoformat()\n                if self._last_failure_timestamp else None,\n        }\n\n    async def reset_first_failure_state(self) -> None:\n        \"\"\"Re-arm detection (adc-65l3; adc-12bt §7.5). Keeps counters for diagnostics.\"\"\"\n        async with self._first_failure_lock:\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n            # _failure_count and _last_failure_timestamp intentionally retained\n```\n\nWait, there's a subtlety on `_is_reachable = False` inside `_record_failure_locked`. adc-50ld §5.4 says `_is_reachable` is \"explicitly out of scope\" for the lock — meaning we should NOT add lock acquisition to its OTHER write paths (success at :80, health-check). But it doesn't say the failure-path write of `_is_reachable = False` must move out of the locked section. The current code sets `self._is_reachable = False` at the top of `_handle_send_failure`. Since `_record_failure_locked` is the body that now runs under the lock, leaving `_is_reachable = False` there is fine and keeps locality. The \"out of scope\" point is about not forcing the success/health-check paths to take this lock. So I'll keep `_is_reachable = False` inside `_record_failure_locked` but note the rationale. Good — my draft handles this.\n\nActually, let me reconsider. adc-50ld §5.4: \"every write is a single atomic STORE_ATTR with no check-then-act... coupling it to a failure-path lock would force the success/health-check paths to acquire a failure lock for no reason.\" So the concern is purely: don't make OTHER paths take the lock. The failure-path write can happily live under the lock (it's already there). I'll keep it and add a one-line note.
